The Spark Connect protocol spark connect protocol ships unresolved logical plans as protobuf over gRPC — the wire format, not the DSL, is the contract The mental model in one line: the Spark Connect protocol defines a gRPC service whose request payload is an unresolved logical plan serialised as protobuf, and whose response is a stream of Arrow record batches encoding the query result . Once you say "protobuf plan in, Arrow batches out, gRPC carries both," every other protocol question — multi-language clients, plan visibility, streaming results, error handling — becomes a deduction from that contract. The protocol surface — what gRPC services are defined. SparkConnectService — the core service. Main RPCs: ExecutePlan , AnalyzePlan , Config , AddArtifacts , Interrupt , ReattachExecute , ReleaseExecute . ExecutePlan — server-streaming RPC. Client sends one ExecutePlanRequest with the protobuf plan; server streams back ExecutePlanResponse messages, each carrying one Arrow record batch or a control message. AnalyzePlan — unary RPC for .printSchema() , .explain() , .columns — schema and plan inspection without execution. Config — unary RPC for spark.conf.get / set / unset . Per-session config lives on the server. AddArtifacts — bidirectional streaming. Uploads JARs, Python files, archives into the session classpath. Interrupt — unary RPC to cancel an in-flight ExecutePlan (the Ctrl+C story). Anatomy of an unresolved logical plan. The plan is a protobuf message named Relation . Every DataFrame operation in the client builds a tree of Relation nodes: Read , Project , Filter , Aggregate , Join , Sort , Limit , Sample , WithColumn , etc. Each Relation carries its operands (sub-relations) and its parameters (column expressions, predicates, join conditions). Expressions are themselves a tree ( Expression protobuf with variants like Literal , UnresolvedAttribute , UnresolvedFunction , Cast , BinaryOp ). "Unresolved" means the client has not bound column names to actual schema fields or function names to actual implementations. That binding happens server-side during the Analyzer phase of Catalyst. This is critical: the client does not need a schema to build a plan. It can reference df["user_id"] without knowing whether user_id is a string or a long — the server resolves it when the plan is executed. The execution path on the server. The server receives the protobuf plan and constructs an internal LogicalPlan from it (the same LogicalPlan data structure the classic Spark driver uses). The plan flows through Catalyst: Analyzer → CacheManager → Optimizer → SparkPlanner → physical plan with AQE rules. The physical plan executes on the server's existing executor pool. Arrow record batches are streamed back to the client via the ExecutePlan response stream. The session has its own SQL config, its own temp views, its own UDFs, its own catalog cache. The server multiplexes many sessions on top of the same executor pool. The Arrow result format — why batches matter. Results are streamed as Apache Arrow record batches. Each ExecutePlanResponse carries either zero or one Arrow batch in its arrow_batch field. Arrow's columnar format is portable across languages — the Python client deserialises directly into a pyarrow.Table or pandas.DataFrame ; the Scala client gets a Dataset[Row] ; the Go client gets an Arrow record reader. Batch sizes are configurable via spark.connect.grpc.arrow.maxBatchSize (default ~4 MB). Larger batches reduce gRPC framing overhead; smaller batches reduce time-to-first-byte for .show() previews. The Arrow stream may also carry a Metrics message at the end (query metrics, plan execution time, executor stats) and an ExecutionProgress message during long-running queries (for .show_progress() -style UI). Plan introspection — what the client can see locally. A Connect client can call .explain(mode="extended") which sends an AnalyzePlan request. The server returns the analysed plan, the optimised plan, and the physical plan as protobuf, and the client formats them back to text locally. A Connect client can call .printSchema() which sends an AnalyzePlan request with mode SCHEMA . The server returns the schema protobuf; the client formats it. The client cannot see the executor topology, the partition count, or per-task statistics directly — those live on the server. For deep observability, point a Spark UI or a metrics scraper at the server. Common interview probes on the protocol. "What is on the wire between a Spark Connect client and the server?" — unresolved logical plan as protobuf, Arrow batches back. "What does the server do with the plan?" — runs Catalyst (Analyzer → Optimizer → SparkPlanner) and executes on its own executor pool. "Why protobuf for the plan?" — language-neutral, schema-evolution-safe, efficient serialisation. Lets any language with a protobuf code generator become a client. "Why Arrow for the result?" — columnar, language-neutral, zero-copy between server JVM and Python / Go / Rust clients via Arrow IPC. Worked example — inspecting a logical plan on the wire Detailed explanation. A common diagnostic skill — capture and inspect the protobuf plan that a Spark Connect client is about to send. This is the "show me what's actually on the wire" exercise senior interviewers love because it forces you to demonstrate that you understand the protocol, not just the DSL. Question. Given a small PySpark Connect program that filters and groups a DataFrame, show how to inspect the protobuf plan that the client builds before it goes over gRPC. What is in the plan? Input. Operation Expected protobuf node spark.read.parquet(...) Relation.read (DataSource) .filter(df.x > 10) Relation.filter .groupBy("y").count() Relation.aggregate Code. from pyspark.sql import SparkSession spark = SparkSession . builder . remote ( " sc://connect:15002 " ). getOrCreate () df = spark . read . parquet ( " s3://lake/events/ " ) result = df . filter ( df . x > 10 ). groupBy ( " y " ). count () # Inspect the protobuf plan the client would send plan_proto = result . _plan . to_proto ( spark . client ) print ( type ( plan_proto ). __name__ ) print ( plan_proto ) # human-readable protobuf text format Step-by-step explanation. Every DataFrame operation in the Connect client returns a new DataFrame whose _plan attribute is a tree of LogicalPlan proto nodes. The leaf is a Read node referencing the parquet path; each subsequent operation wraps the previous plan in a new node. Calling to_proto(client) materialises the in-memory tree into a Relation protobuf message. This is the exact object that goes inside an ExecutePlanRequest.plan.root field when the client invokes the gRPC ExecutePlan method. The printed protobuf shows three nested Relation messages: the outermost is the aggregate, its input is a filter, and the filter's input is a read. Inside the aggregate are the grouping expressions ( UnresolvedAttribute("y") ) and aggregate expressions ( UnresolvedFunction("count", []) ). The expressions are themselves protobuf trees. df.x > 10 becomes BinaryOp(operator="greaterthan", left=UnresolvedAttribute("x"), right=Literal(10, integer)) . Nothing is resolved yet — the client does not need the schema to build this. When .collect() or .show() is called, the client packages this plan into an ExecutePlanRequest , opens a gRPC stream, and waits for ExecutePlanResponse messages. The first response typically carries the result schema; subsequent responses carry Arrow batches; the final response carries query metrics. Output. Plan node Fields Relation.aggregate grouping= [UnresolvedAttribute("y")] , agg= [count()] , input= filter Relation.filter predicate= x > 10 , input= read Relation.read format= parquet , paths= [s3://lake/events/] Rule of thumb. Whenever you debug a Connect client misbehaviour, dump the protobuf plan first. If the plan looks right but the result is wrong, the bug is server-side (Catalyst, executor); if the plan looks wrong, the bug is client-side (your DSL call constructed the wrong tree). Worked example — Arrow batches streaming back Detailed explanation. A common interview question — "you call .collect() on a 10 GB DataFrame against a Spark Connect server. What happens between the client and the server?" The answer demonstrates the result-side of the protocol: the gRPC server-streaming RPC and Arrow batch chunking. Question. Trace what happens when a Connect client calls .collect() on a result with 100 million rows. Highlight the streaming behaviour and how the client controls memory. Input. Aspect Value Result rows 100M Arrow batch size 10K rows (~4 MB compressed) Network gRPC server-streaming Client memory budget 1 GB Code. # What `collect()` actually does under the hood, simplified from pyspark.sql import SparkSession spark = SparkSession . builder . remote ( " sc://connect:15002 " ). getOrCreate () df = spark . read . parquet ( " s3://lake/big-events/ " ). filter ( " status = ' OK '" ) # Memory-safe: stream results into pyarrow Table import pyarrow as pa batches = [] for batch in df . _explain_plan_stream (): # pseudo-code; real API is iterator-based batches . append ( batch ) # each batch is ~4 MB table = pa . Table . from_batches ( batches ) print ( f " Got { len ( batches ) } batches, total rows { table . num_rows } " ) # Less safe: pull everything into memory at once rows = df . collect () # OOM if result is large Step-by-step explanation. The client sends an ExecutePlanRequest to the server's ExecutePlan gRPC method. This is a server-streaming RPC: one request, many responses. The server keeps the stream open until the query completes (or is interrupted). The server begins executing the query. As executor tasks finish and return results, the server's connect handler accumulates them into Arrow record batches sized to spark.connect.grpc.arrow.maxBatchSize (default ~4 MB). Each batch becomes one ExecutePlanResponse message. The server flushes the message to the gRPC channel; HTTP/2 frames carry it to the client. The server never holds the full result in memory — backpressure is provided by gRPC flow control (the client must consume batches as fast as it can or the server will pause). The client receives each ExecutePlanResponse , deserialises the Arrow batch, and either accumulates it ( .collect() builds a full table) or yields it to user code ( .toLocalIterator() streams batch-by-batch). For a 100M-row result, the naive .collect() builds an in-memory list of 100M rows — possibly tens of GB. Use .toLocalIterator() or .toArrow() with an iterator-based consumer if the result is large. The client controls maximum result size with spark.connect.grpc.maxInboundMessageSize . If the result exceeds the limit, the client raises a gRPC RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED error and aborts. Output. Phase Bytes on the wire ExecutePlanRequest (plan only) ~5-50 KB Per ExecutePlanResponse (Arrow batch) ~4 MB (configurable) Total responses for 100M rows ~10,000 batches Total wire bytes ~40 GB (compressed Arrow) Rule of thumb. For result sets larger than a few hundred MB, prefer .toLocalIterator() or .write.parquet(...) over .collect() . The Connect protocol can stream any size, but the client memory is bounded by its host environment. Worked example — multi-language gRPC client over the same protocol Detailed explanation. The "same protocol, different client languages" demonstration. Show that a Go service and a Python notebook can both hit the same Spark Connect Server using the same wire format, just with different code-generated stubs. Question. Show how a Python client and a Go client both run the same query against the same Connect Server. Highlight the shared protocol and the language-specific stub. Input. Client Stub source Wire format Python pyspark[connect] gRPC + protobuf + Arrow Go spark-connect-go (community) gRPC + protobuf + Arrow Scala spark-connect-client-jvm gRPC + protobuf + Arrow Code. # Python client from pyspark.sql import SparkSession spark = SparkSession . builder . remote ( " sc://connect:15002 " ). getOrCreate () df = spark . sql ( " SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) AS n FROM events GROUP BY user_id " ) df . show ( 5 ) // Go client (community SDK) package main import ( "context" "fmt" sc "github.com/apache/spark-connect-go/v4/spark/sql" ) func main () { ctx := context . Background () spark , _ := sc . NewSessionBuilder () . Remote ( "sc://connect:15002" ) . Build ( ctx ) df , _ := spark . Sql ( ctx , "SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) AS n FROM events GROUP BY user_id" ) rows , _ := df . Collect ( ctx ) for _ , r := range rows [ : 5 ] { fmt . Println ( r ) } } Step-by-step explanation. Both clients open a gRPC channel to connect:15002 . The channel is plain HTTP/2 with optional TLS; gRPC headers carry the user/session metadata. Both clients build the same query — a SQL string. SQL is the simplest payload: an ExecutePlanRequest whose plan root is a Relation.sql node carrying the SQL text. The server is identical in both cases. It runs Catalyst, executes the query, and streams Arrow batches back. The server has no idea whether the client is Python, Go, Scala, or Rust — it sees only protobuf bytes. Each client decodes the Arrow batches using its language's Arrow library: Python via pyarrow , Go via arrow-go , Scala via the JVM Arrow library. The decode produces idiomatic typed objects for the host language. Result: the same query, two clients, one protocol. The cost-of-adding-a-language is "write a gRPC + Arrow client" — a weekend, not a quarter. Output. Aspect Python client Go client Install pip install pyspark[connect] go get spark-connect-go Wire format identical identical Server cost identical identical Result type pyspark Row / pandas Go struct / Arrow record Rule of thumb. Adding a new client language is bounded work because the protocol is one gRPC + protobuf definition. If your team needs Spark queries from a non-Python service, prefer building a thin Spark Connect client over wrapping a process-level shell-out to spark-submit . Senior interview question on the Connect protocol A senior interviewer might ask: "Explain what happens to a PySpark Connect program between the moment a user calls .collect() and the moment the rows show up in the client. What graphs get built, what RPCs happen, and what is the role of each component?" Solution Using the build → encode → execute → stream pipeline Client side ----------- 1. User builds DataFrame → in-memory tree of LogicalPlan proto nodes 2. .collect() triggers to_proto() → ExecutePlanRequest(plan=root) 3. gRPC channel opened (TLS optional) → server-streaming ExecutePlan RPC Server side (Spark Connect Server) ---------------------------------- 4. Connect handler receives ExecutePlanRequest, looks up SparkSession by user_id + session_id 5. Protobuf plan → internal LogicalPlan 6. Analyzer (UnresolvedRelation → ResolvedRelation), Optimizer, SparkPlanner 7. Physical plan executes on existing executor pool, producing rows 8. Rows batched into Arrow record batches; each batch wrapped in ExecutePlanResponse 9. Server streams responses to client over the gRPC stream Client side (continued) ---------------------- 10. Client receives each ExecutePlanResponse, decodes Arrow batch 11. Accumulates into pandas / list of Row depending on call 12. Stream completes (final response carries metrics) → returns to user Step-by-step trace. Stage Where Output 1 Client process LogicalPlan proto tree 2 Client process ExecutePlanRequest 3 Client → server gRPC stream opened 4 Server connect handler SparkSession resolved 5 Server Internal LogicalPlan 6 Server (Catalyst) Physical plan 7 Server executor pool Result rows 8 Server Arrow record batches 9 Server → client gRPC stream of ExecutePlanResponse 10 Client Decoded Arrow batches 11 Client pandas / list of Row 12 Client Returned to user The pipeline distinguishes the protocol from the engine. The Spark Connect Server is just "the part of Spark that speaks gRPC + protobuf + Arrow"; everything below it (Catalyst, executors, AQE) is the same Spark as classic. Output: Component Owns Talks to Connect client LogicalPlan proto tree → ExecutePlanRequest Connect Server (gRPC) Connect Server handler Per-session SparkSession + plan deserialiser Driver core (in-process) Driver core (Catalyst) Analyzer + Optimizer + Planner Cluster scheduler Cluster scheduler Task allocation Executors (RPC) Executors Task execution + Arrow encoding Connect Server (response stream) Why this works — concept by concept: Three layers, three concerns — the Connect client owns the plan tree ; the Connect Server owns the session ; the underlying Spark driver core owns the execution . Each layer is a refinement; you do not jump straight from DSL to executors. Protobuf is the contract, Arrow is the result format — both are language-neutral, version-stable, and zero-copy where possible. The contract is what lets you swap clients without touching the server. Server-streaming RPC carries the result — one request triggers many responses, with gRPC flow control providing backpressure. The server never has to buffer the full result in memory. Catalyst runs on the server, not the client — the client builds an unresolved plan; the server resolves and optimises it. This is why the client does not need the schema to build a query. Cost — protocol overhead is a single round-trip per ExecutePlan plus per-batch streaming. For interactive workloads, this is dominated by the actual query cost; for batch jobs with millions of plan executions, classic Spark's in-process dispatch wins. Multi-language clients spark connect python is one client of many — the protocol lets any gRPC-capable language drive the same remote SparkSession The mental model in one line: a Spark Connect client is any program that speaks the protocol — a small protobuf-defined gRPC contract — so PySpark, Scala, Go, Rust, and Node clients are all peers, none of them special, all built against the same generated stubs . Once you say "client = protocol implementation, server = Spark engine," the entire pyspark connect vs Scala vs Go interview surface becomes a deduction from "everyone speaks the same protobuf." The official + community client matrix in 2026. PySpark Connect ( pyspark[connect] ) — the most polished client. Pure Python; no JVM dependency. Same DataFrame / Column / GroupedData API as classic PySpark. Pandas API on Spark also supported. Spark Connect Scala client ( spark-connect-client-jvm ) — pure-JVM client, no SparkContext required. Ships its own typed Dataset API. Spark Connect Go ( spark-connect-go , community) — gRPC + Arrow, idiomatic Go. Maps DataFrames to Arrow records or Go structs. Spark Connect Rust ( spark-connect-rs , community) — async Tokio-based; returns Arrow record batches. Spark Connect Node.js ( spark-connect-node , community) — uses the official gRPC libraries; integrates with TypeScript types generated from the protobuf. Spark Connect R (planned / experimental) — narrows the long-standing sparklyr complexity. What's identical across clients. The protobuf plan tree, the gRPC method names, the Arrow result format, and the per-session isolation model — every client speaks the same wire contract. What's different across clients. API ergonomics. PySpark Connect reads almost identically to classic PySpark; Scala looks like Dataset[Row] ; Go uses df.Collect(ctx) ; Rust returns Result<arrow::RecordBatch, Error> . UDF support. Python clients ship Python UDFs (Python workers on server). Scala clients ship Scala UDFs via AddArtifacts JAR upload. Go and Rust are SQL-only for custom logic. Streaming support. Structured Streaming over Connect is best in PySpark and Scala; Go and Rust support is partial in 2026. The "no SparkContext, only SparkSession" reality. The classic SparkContext is a JVM object owning the cluster topology, broadcast variables, accumulators, and the RDD API. It does not exist on the client side in Spark Connect. A Connect client gets only SparkSession . From SparkSession you can do everything in the DataFrame and SQL API, plus session-scoped configs and temp views. This is a feature, not a bug: it forces the API surface to be "things that can be expressed as a logical plan." Anything that requires direct access to executors (RDD ops, accumulators) lives on the server, not the client. Existing code that reaches for sc.parallelize , sc.accumulator , sc.broadcast , etc., needs to be rewritten or kept on classic Spark. API parity table — Connect vs classic (high level). Supported on Connect — DataFrame API, SQL API, Pandas API on Spark, Structured Streaming, Catalog API (most operations), built-in functions, Python UDFs (via worker on server), Pandas UDFs, Scala UDFs (via uploaded JAR), Iceberg / Delta sinks via DataSourceV2. Not supported on Connect — RDD API, SparkContext directly, accumulators, broadcast variables explicitly created via sc.broadcast , some legacy org.apache.spark.sql.functions overloads with Java-class-of arguments, classic UDFs registered via JavaSparkContext , JavaRDD . Partially supported — some Streaming sources / sinks (improving fast in 4.x), some catalog admin operations (CREATE VIEW IF NOT EXISTS, table properties), JDBC operations needing direct executor access. Catalog access — what Connect can and cannot do. spark.catalog.listDatabases() , listTables() , listColumns() , createTable() , cacheTable() , uncacheTable() — all supported via the Catalog API. Iceberg CALL procedures, Delta OPTIMIZE , table-management SQL — all supported via plain spark.sql(...) . Direct calls into the HiveSessionCatalog or SessionCatalog Scala objects — not supported, by design. Common interview probes on the client matrix. "Why does Spark Connect not expose SparkContext on the client?" — the protocol is a logical-plan + result-stream contract; SparkContext is an executor-graph object that has no meaning on the client side. "Can a Go service ship a UDF to a Spark Connect Server?" — no; only Python (worker-based) and JVM (JAR-based) UDFs can ship. Go and Rust clients are SQL-only for custom logic. "What does pip install pyspark[connect] ship vs pip install pyspark ?" — [connect] is a slim pure-Python wheel; plain pyspark ships the full JVM Spark distribution. "Can a single client switch between classic and Connect modes?" — yes; SparkSession.builder accepts both .master(...) (classic) and .remote(...) (Connect); the rest of the code is identical for DataFrame / SQL work. Worked example — switching a PySpark script from classic to Connect Detailed explanation. A typical migration scenario — take a classic PySpark batch job, convert it to a Connect-friendly script that runs against a long-lived Connect Server. What changes is two lines; what stays the same is the rest of the code. Question. Convert a classic PySpark job to PySpark Connect. Highlight what changes, what stays the same, and the single class of code that does not migrate. Input. Concern Classic Connect Session builder .master("yarn") .remote("sc://...") Client install pyspark==4.0.0 (400 MB) pyspark[connect]==4.0.0 (10 MB) RDD code works rewrite needed DataFrame / SQL code works works (unchanged) Code. # Classic PySpark batch job from pyspark.sql import SparkSession from pyspark.sql.functions import col , count spark = SparkSession . builder . appName ( " daily-counts " ). master ( " yarn " ). getOrCreate () df = spark . read . parquet ( " s3://lake/events/dt=2026-06-21/ " ) result = df . filter ( col ( " status " ) == " OK " ). groupBy ( " user_id " ). agg ( count ( " * " ). alias ( " n " )) result . write . parquet ( " s3://lake/agg/user-daily/dt=2026-06-21/ " ) spark . stop () # Same job on PySpark Connect — only the session builder changed from pyspark.sql import SparkSession from pyspark.sql.functions import col , count spark = SparkSession . builder \ . remote ( " sc://spark-connect.platform:15002 " ) \ . getOrCreate () df = spark . read . parquet ( " s3://lake/events/dt=2026-06-21/ " ) result = df . filter ( col ( " status " ) == " OK " ). groupBy ( " user_id " ). agg ( count ( " * " ). alias ( " n " )) result . write . parquet ( " s3://lake/agg/user-daily/dt=2026-06-21/ " ) spark . stop () Step-by-step explanation. The session builder swaps .master("yarn") for .remote("sc://...") . The rest of the script is identical — the DataFrame API is shared. This is the typical Connect migration: two lines. The job still reads parquet, filters, groups, writes back to S3. All of these operations are server-side after the plan is shipped, so they execute identically. The client runs in a tiny Python process (the pyspark[connect] wheel). No JVM, no SPARK_HOME , no spark-submit . A cron job or Airflow operator can launch this script in a tiny container. The one class of code that does not migrate cleanly: any reference to spark.sparkContext (or sc ). If your script does sc.parallelize([1,2,3]) , you need to rewrite that as spark.createDataFrame([(1,),(2,),(3,)], ["x"]) or keep the script on classic Spark. The performance is essentially identical for DataFrame workloads — Catalyst still optimises the plan; the executors still do the work. The only added cost is the one-time plan ship + final Arrow streaming, both of which are tiny compared to the actual S3 read. Output. Migration step Effort Replace .master() with .remote() 1 minute per script Replace sc.parallelize(...) with spark.createDataFrame(...) per call, 1-5 minutes Replace sc.broadcast(...) with spark.sparkContext.broadcast(...) (unsupported) rewrite using join Replace sc.accumulator(...) with aggregation queries rewrite as groupBy().agg() Rule of thumb. Most PySpark batch jobs migrate to Connect in under an hour. The migration cost is real only for jobs that genuinely use RDD-side APIs — and those should be considered for full rewrite anyway. Worked example — a Go service driving a Spark Connect query Detailed explanation. A common architecture question — "your Go-based API service needs to run an aggregate query against a Spark cluster. How would you wire it up?" The answer demonstrates the multi-language strength of Spark Connect. Question. Show how a Go HTTP service queries a Spark Connect Server. Highlight the gRPC client, the query, and the Arrow result decoding. Input. Component Role HTTP service accepts a GET /sales/by-region request Spark Connect Server runs the aggregation on Spark Result JSON response to the HTTP caller Code. package main import ( "context" "encoding/json" "log" "net/http" sc "github.com/apache/spark-connect-go/v4/spark/sql" ) type Row struct { Region string `json:"region"` Total float64 `json:"total"` } func main () { ctx := context . Background () spark , err := sc . NewSessionBuilder () . Remote ( "sc://spark-connect:15002" ) . Build ( ctx ) if err != nil { log . Fatal ( err ) } http . HandleFunc ( "/sales/by-region" , func ( w http . ResponseWriter , r * http . Request ) { df , _ := spark . Sql ( ctx , `SELECT region, SUM(amount) AS total FROM sales WHERE dt = current_date() GROUP BY region` ) records , _ := df . Collect ( ctx ) out := make ([] Row , 0 , len ( records )) for _ , rec := range records { out = append ( out , Row { Region : rec . Value ( 0 ) . ( string ), Total : rec . Value ( 1 ) . ( float64 ), }) } json . NewEncoder ( w ) . Encode ( out ) }) log . Fatal ( http . ListenAndServe ( ":8080" , nil )) } Step-by-step explanation. The Go service depends on spark-connect-go , a community-maintained gRPC + Arrow client. Adding it is a go get — no JVM, no Spark distribution, no Py4J. The service builds a long-lived SparkSession at process start. The session opens a gRPC channel to the Connect Server; under the hood this is HTTP/2 with optional TLS. Each HTTP request triggers spark.Sql(...) which builds a Relation.sql protobuf plan and ships it via ExecutePlan . The server runs the same Catalyst pipeline as it would for a Python or Scala client. The server streams Arrow record batches back; the Go client decodes them into Go-native typed values (string, float64, etc.) using the Arrow Go library. The service marshals to JSON and writes the HTTP response. Critically: the Go service holds no Spark state. The session lives on the server. If the service restarts, it reconnects to the server. The server is the durable component. Output. Aspect Value Service install size ~10 MB (Go binary) Spark dependencies none in the Go binary Per-request latency ~50-200 ms (driver-side query time dominates) Throughput bounded by server-side query concurrency, not Go service Rule of thumb. If your team has a non-JVM service that needs Spark analytics, prefer a Connect client over a process-level spark-submit shell-out. The protocol is built for exactly this case. Worked example — Python UDFs on Spark Connect Detailed explanation. A common probe — "Python UDFs are a classic PySpark feature. How do they work over Spark Connect, given that the Python client and the JVM driver are now in different processes?" The answer reveals one of the subtler engineering wins. Question. Show a Pandas UDF defined in a PySpark Connect client and explain how it executes on the server. Input. Aspect Value UDF type Pandas UDF (Series → Series) Client PySpark Connect (no JVM locally) Server Spark Connect Server with Python worker pool Code. from pyspark.sql import SparkSession from pyspark.sql.functions import pandas_udf from pyspark.sql.types import StringType import pandas as pd spark = SparkSession . builder . remote ( " sc://connect:15002 " ). getOrCreate () @pandas_udf ( StringType ()) def upper_pd ( s : pd . Series ) -> pd . Series : return s . str . upper () df = spark . read . parquet ( " s3://lake/users/ " ) result = df . withColumn ( " name_upper " , upper_pd ( df . name )) result . show ( 5 ) Step-by-step explanation. The @pandas_udf decorator captures the user's Python function upper_pd . When the DataFrame operation upper_pd(df.name) is called, the client serialises the function (via cloudpickle), packages it into a protobuf CommonInlineUserDefinedFunction node, and inserts it into the plan tree. The plan, including the serialised UDF, is shipped to the server via ExecutePlan . The server runs Catalyst on it; the UDF appears as a leaf expression that requires a Python evaluator. The server runs the query and, for partitions that need the UDF, sends data into a Python worker process (the same Python-worker model classic PySpark uses). The worker receives the pickled function bytes, unpickles, runs the function on each Arrow batch, sends results back. The Python workers run on the executor nodes — not on the Connect client machine. The client only ships the function definition; execution happens server-side, distributed across executors. Net effect: Python UDFs work exactly like classic PySpark, with one subtle difference — the UDF code is round-tripped from the client to the server as part of the plan, instead of being on the driver's local classpath. Heavy dependencies should be pre-installed in the server's Python environment, not shipped with each query. Output. Aspect Behaviour UDF definition client-side, decorated with @pandas_udf UDF serialisation cloudpickle, embedded in plan protobuf UDF execution server-side Python worker on each executor Heavy deps pre-install on server, not on client Rule of thumb. Python UDFs in Connect are "the same as classic PySpark, with serialisation happening through the protocol." Keep UDF dependencies small enough to serialise quickly; install heavy libraries (NumPy, scikit-learn, transformers) into the server's Python environment at deploy time. Senior interview question on the client matrix A senior interviewer might ask: "You have a multi-language team — Python data scientists, a Scala backend, and a Go service layer — all of whom need to issue Spark queries against the same cluster. How does Spark Connect change your architecture compared to classic Spark?" Solution Using a shared Connect Server + per-language clients Architecture sketch =================== ┌─────────────────────┐ │ Spark Connect Server│ (one cluster, many clients) │ + Catalyst + AQE │ │ + executor pool │ └──────────┬──────────┘ │ gRPC + protobuf + Arrow ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐ │ │ │ ┌─────▼─────┐ ┌─────▼─────┐ ┌─────▼─────┐ │ Python │ │ Scala │ │ Go │ │ notebook │ │ backend │ │ service │ │ pyspark[ │ │ spark- │ │ spark- │ │ connect] │ │ connect- │ │ connect- │ │ │ │ client-jvm│ │ go │ └───────────┘ └───────────┘ └───────────┘ Step-by-step trace. Step Action Result 1 Deploy single Connect Server on K8s one cluster handles all clients 2 Python notebooks install pyspark[connect] ~10 MB wheel per notebook env 3 Scala backend uses spark-connect-client-jvm standard JVM JAR dependency 4 Go service uses spark-connect-go gRPC + Arrow Go module 5 All clients open gRPC sessions to the same server per-session catalogs isolated 6 Server multiplexes across the shared executor pool one cluster cost, many users After the wiring, the team has one Spark cluster and three first-class language clients. Compared to classic Spark, the savings are: no Livy / JobServer shim, no separate Spark distribution per language, no JVM cost on the client side, no Py4J for non-Python languages. Output: Concern Classic Spark Spark Connect Clusters per language one per pattern (Spark+JVM, Spark+Py4J shim) one Connect cluster total Per-language client install full Spark distro slim per-language client Multi-tenant story Livy / custom job server first-class (per-session isolation) New-language cost weeks to months (JVM bridge) days (gRPC + Arrow stub) Why this works — concept by concept: One protocol, many clients — once the gRPC + protobuf + Arrow contract is fixed, every language is a stub-generation job, not a JVM-integration project. New languages join the cluster cheaply. Per-session isolation comes for free — the server assigns a session ID per client and isolates catalogs and configs. Classic Spark needs a Livy or JobServer for the same shape. No JVM on the client — the Python, Go, Rust clients are pure native code. They run in Lambdas, Cloud Run, edge containers, mobile, anywhere with a gRPC library. UDF surface differs by client — Python clients can ship Python UDFs, JVM clients can ship Scala UDFs, native clients are SQL-only for custom logic. Plan for this when picking the client language for UDF-heavy work. Cost — adding a new client language is O(stub generation); adding a new cluster per language is O(months). Multi-language teams are the textbook win for Spark Connect. Session isolation + multi-tenancy A shared spark connect server multiplexes many sessions onto one cluster — catalog and configs isolate; compute does not (by default) The mental model in one line: a Spark Connect Server hosts many SparkSessions concurrently, each identified by (user_id, session_id) , each with its own catalog and configs but sharing the underlying executor pool — so logical isolation is first-class while compute isolation requires explicit scheduler-pool design . Once you say "logical per session, compute shared by default," every multi-tenancy interview question becomes a sequence of consequences from that split. Session identity and lifecycle. A new client call to SparkSession.builder.remote(...).getOrCreate() opens a gRPC channel and sends a Config or ExecutePlan request with user_id and session_id headers. If the (user_id, session_id) pair is new, the server creates a fresh SparkSession via SparkSession.cloneSession() (Spark's built-in session cloning facility) — fast, in-process, no new driver JVM. The session persists in the server's session registry until either an explicit Close (rare) or the configured idle timeout passes. Reconnecting a client with the same (user_id, session_id) finds the same session, with its caches, temp views, and configs intact. What is isolated per session. Temp views — df.createOrReplaceTempView("v") registers v in the session catalog only. Other sessions cannot see it. SQL config — spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "200") is a per-session knob; doesn't leak. Catalog current database / schema — spark.catalog.setCurrentDatabase("analytics") is per-session. Registered UDFs — spark.udf.register("my_fn", ...) registers in the session's UDF registry, not the server-wide one. Cached DataFrames — df.cache() caches relative to the session; other sessions cannot reuse the same logical plan's cache by name. What is shared across sessions (by default). Executor pool — all sessions on a Connect Server share the underlying executor allocation. A heavy query in one session contends for executor slots with all other sessions. Shared catalog (Hive Metastore, Iceberg REST catalog, Unity Catalog) — the persistent catalog is server-wide; sessions read and write the same tables. Server-level SparkConf — spark.executor.memory , spark.driver.memory , network configs — these are set at server boot and apply globally. Cluster scheduler resources — slots, dynamic allocation, queues. By default, sessions compete in the same FIFO scheduler pool. Compute isolation patterns — making sessions fair. Spark Scheduler Pools. Configure spark.scheduler.allocation.file to define named pools (e.g. interactive , batch , etl ) with fair-scheduler weights. Each session can opt into a pool via spark.scheduler.pool . Heavy batch users can be isolated from interactive analysts. Dynamic allocation per pool. With Kubernetes-managed Spark, each pool can have its own min/max executor cap. Combined with Spark's dynamicAllocation , you get loose CPU isolation. Server per tenant. For strict isolation, run one Spark Connect Server per tenant (one per team or per business unit). Trades operational cost for clean isolation. Common for enterprise / regulated workloads. Server per workload class. Run a connect-interactive server for notebooks and a connect-etl server for scheduled batch. Each has its own cluster sizing and queue depth. Authentication and authorization patterns. TLS at the gRPC layer. Spark Connect supports TLS via spark.connect.grpc.server.useTransportSecurity=true and certificate config. Required for any production deployment outside a fully trusted network. Interceptor-based auth. The Connect Server supports custom gRPC interceptors via spark.connect.authenticate.token (token-based) or a custom Java interceptor class. Used to validate bearer tokens (OAuth, JWT) or mTLS client certs. User identity propagation. The interceptor sets the user_id on the session, which downstream Spark code can read for catalog ACL enforcement (e.g., Unity Catalog, Apache Ranger). Server-side user impersonation. For Hive Metastore and HDFS access, the server can impersonate the client user via Hadoop's UserGroupInformation — preserving filesystem ACLs. The "shared pool, fair scheduler" recipe (most common production pattern). Configure spark.scheduler.mode=FAIR at server boot. Define a fairscheduler.xml with named pools ( interactive , etl , default ). Per client, set spark.scheduler.pool via spark.conf.set(...) early in the session. The fair scheduler now balances tasks across pools by weight; heavy ETL queries no longer starve interactive notebooks. Common interview probes on multi-tenancy. "What is isolated between two sessions on a Connect Server?" — catalog/temp views/configs/UDFs/caches per session. Executor pool shared. "How do you enforce CPU isolation between sessions?" — Spark scheduler pools + fair scheduler + per-pool dynamic allocation. "When would you run one server per tenant?" — regulated workloads, strict cost attribution, when one tenant's heavy query risk would block others under fair scheduling. "How do clients authenticate to the Connect Server?" — TLS + token interceptor (custom gRPC interceptor); user identity propagated to Spark session for catalog ACLs. Worked example — two notebook users on one server Detailed explanation. A common scenario — two data analysts run notebooks pointing at the same Spark Connect Server. Show that their catalogs and configs do not collide. Question. Demonstrate that two PySpark Connect notebooks sharing the same Connect Server have independent sessions, with isolated temp views and configs. Input. Notebook user_id session_id Action Alice alice A1 create temp view recent_sales , set shuffle.partitions=400 Bob bob B1 create temp view recent_sales (different content), default shuffle Code. # Alice's notebook from pyspark.sql import SparkSession spark = SparkSession . builder \ . remote ( " sc://connect:15002 " ) \ . config ( " spark.connect.session.user_id " , " alice " ) \ . getOrCreate () spark . conf . set ( " spark.sql.shuffle.partitions " , " 400 " ) df = spark . read . parquet ( " s3://lake/orders/dt=2026-06-21/ " ) df . createOrReplaceTempView ( " recent_sales " ) print ( spark . sql ( " SELECT COUNT(*) FROM recent_sales " ). collect ()) # Bob's notebook — same server, different session from pyspark.sql import SparkSession spark = SparkSession . builder \ . remote ( " sc://connect:15002 " ) \ . config ( " spark.connect.session.user_id " , " bob " ) \ . getOrCreate () # Bob's shuffle.partitions is the server default — Alice's 400 doesn't leak print ( spark . conf . get ( " spark.sql.shuffle.partitions " )) # Bob's view "recent_sales" is independent of Alice's df = spark . read . parquet ( " s3://lake/returns/dt=2026-06-21/ " ) df . createOrReplaceTempView ( " recent_sales " ) print ( spark . sql ( " SELECT COUNT(*) FROM recent_sales " ). collect ()) # different count Step-by-step explanation. Each client's user_id is propagated via gRPC headers (or a custom interceptor in production). The server reads this on every ExecutePlan request. On Alice's first call, the server's session registry has no entry for (alice, A1) , so it clones a fresh SparkSession from the base session and caches it. Alice's spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "400") calls the gRPC Config method; the server updates Alice's session's SQL config only. Bob's first call similarly creates a (bob, B1) session. His spark.conf.get("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions") returns the server default (e.g. 200), unaffected by Alice's 400. Both notebooks call createOrReplaceTempView("recent_sales") — but the temp view registry is per session. Each query against recent_sales resolves to the calling session's view. Output. Aspect Alice Bob spark.sql.shuffle.partitions 400 200 (default) recent_sales count from orders from returns Temp views [recent_sales] (orders) [recent_sales] (returns) Rule of thumb. Logical isolation is first-class on Spark Connect; you do not need to design for it explicitly. Compute isolation needs an explicit scheduler-pool design — covered below. Worked example — scheduler pools for compute fairness Detailed explanation. A common production probe — "your Connect Server has 50 notebook users and 5 scheduled ETL jobs. The ETL jobs hog the executor pool and notebooks crawl. Fix it." The answer is the Spark fair scheduler with named pools. Question. Configure the Spark Connect Server with two scheduler pools ( interactive and etl ) and route each client session to the appropriate pool. Walk through what changes. Input. Workload Pool Weight Notebooks (50 users) interactive 3 ETL jobs (5 jobs) etl 1 Code. <!-- fairscheduler.xml — bind in the Connect Server start command --> <?xml version="1.0"?> <allocations> <pool name= "interactive" > <schedulingMode> FAIR </schedulingMode> <weight> 3 </weight> <minShare> 10 </minShare> </pool> <pool name= "etl" > <schedulingMode> FIFO </schedulingMode> <weight> 1 </weight> <minShare> 4 </minShare> </pool> </allocations> # Connect Server boot config spark-connect-server \ --conf spark.scheduler.mode = FAIR \ --conf spark.scheduler.allocation.file = /opt/spark/conf/fairscheduler.xml # Per-session pool assignment in a notebook spark = SparkSession . builder . remote ( " sc://connect:15002 " ). getOrCreate () spark . conf . set ( " spark.scheduler.pool " , " interactive " ) # opt this session into pool # ... queries from this notebook now run in the 'interactive' pool Step-by-step explanation. At boot, the Connect Server reads fairscheduler.xml and registers the two named pools. Each pool has a weight (how much of the executor pool it gets when contended) and a minShare (the minimum number of slots reserved for it). On each ExecutePlan request, the server looks up the calling session's spark.scheduler.pool config. If set to interactive , tasks for that query go into the interactive pool's task queue. Within a pool, scheduling is either FAIR (round-robin per job) or FIFO (first-come-first-served). interactive uses FAIR so notebook users do not starve each other; etl uses FIFO so each ETL job runs to completion in order. When contention occurs (more tasks queued than slots available), the fair scheduler distributes slots in proportion to pool weights: with weights 3:1, the interactive pool gets 75% of slots and the ETL pool gets 25%. The minShare=10 guarantee on the interactive pool ensures at least 10 executor slots are always reserved for notebook users — even if ETL jobs would otherwise consume the cluster. Output. Metric Without pools With pools Notebook p99 latency under ETL load 60+ sec <5 sec ETL completion time unchanged +10-15% (fair share) Executor slot reserved for notebooks 0 10 (minShare) Operational complexity low medium (one XML file) Rule of thumb. Any production Connect Server with more than one workload class should run fair scheduler pools. The configuration cost is one XML file; the latency benefit for interactive users is enormous. Worked example — server-per-tenant for strict isolation Detailed explanation. A common architecture probe — "you have two regulated tenants (e.g., HR and Finance) that cannot share compute. How do you deploy Spark Connect for them?" The answer is server-per-tenant. Question. Sketch a deployment of Spark Connect Servers for two tenants with strict isolation requirements. Compare cost and operational shape. Input. Tenant Constraint Workload HR data residency, no shared compute low Finance SOX audit, no shared compute high Code. # Two separate Spark Connect Server deployments on Kubernetes apiVersion : apps/v1 kind : Deployment metadata : name : spark-connect-hr namespace : hr spec : replicas : 1 template : spec : containers : - name : connect-server image : registry/spark:4.0.0 args : [ " start-connect-server.sh" , " --conf" , " spark.scheduler.mode=FAIR" ] resources : requests : { cpu : 4 , memory : 16Gi } limits : { cpu : 8 , memory : 32Gi } --- apiVersion : apps/v1 kind : Deployment metadata : name : spark-connect-finance namespace : finance spec : replicas : 1 template : spec : containers : - name : connect-server image : registry/spark:4.0.0 args : [ " start-connect-server.sh" , " --conf" , " spark.scheduler.mode=FAIR" ] resources : requests : { cpu : 16 , memory : 64Gi } limits : { cpu : 64 , memory : 256Gi } Step-by-step explanation. Each tenant has its own Spark Connect Server deployment in its own Kubernetes namespace. No shared executor pool, no shared catalog cache, no shared session registry. HR's clients connect to sc://spark-connect-hr.hr.svc:15002 ; Finance's clients connect to sc://spark-connect-finance.finance.svc:15002 . The two endpoints are wholly independent. Network policies enforce that HR namespace cannot reach Finance namespace at all. Data residency requirements are satisfied because the two clusters never share a process. Operational cost goes up: two deployments to monitor, two upgrade cycles, two cost lines on the bill. For regulated tenants this is acceptable; for general-purpose multi-tenancy, scheduler pools on a shared server are cheaper. SOX audit on Finance becomes much cleaner: one Spark Connect Server, one log stream, one set of running jobs — no cross-tenant logs to filter. Output. Concern One server + pools Server per tenant Cluster cost one cluster total one per tenant (multiplied) Audit scope mixed tenants in logs clean per-tenant boundary Cross-tenant interference possible under heavy load impossible (by construction) Operational overhead one cluster N clusters Rule of thumb. Default to shared server + scheduler pools. Switch to server-per-tenant only when (a) regulatory / compliance requirements forbid shared compute, or (b) one tenant's workload would otherwise dominate fair-share allocation. Both are real, but the former is more common. Senior interview question on multi-tenant Spark Connect A senior interviewer might ask: "You're designing a Spark Connect platform for a 200-person analytics org with notebook users, scheduled ETL, and an ML training workload. Walk me through how you'd lay out servers, scheduler pools, and authentication." Solution Using a 3-server, pooled, token-authenticated architecture Architecture sketch =================== ┌────────────────────────────────┐ │ Identity Provider (OAuth/JWT) │ └────────────┬───────────────────┘ │ tokens to clients ┌──────────────────┼──────────────────────┐ │ │ │ ┌──▼──────────┐ ┌────▼────────┐ ┌──────────▼────────┐ │ Notebook │ │ Airflow ETL │ │ ML training │ │ clients │ │ operators │ │ jobs │ └──┬──────────┘ └────┬────────┘ └──────────┬────────┘ │ │ │ │ gRPC + JWT │ gRPC + JWT │ gRPC + JWT │ │ │ ┌──▼─────────────┐ ┌──▼──────────────┐ ┌─────▼──────────┐ │ connect- │ │ connect-etl │ │ connect-ml │ │ interactive │ │ server │ │ server │ │ server │ │ (FIFO pool) │ │ (large execs) │ │ (FAIR pool) │ │ │ │ │ └────────────────┘ └─────────────────┘ └────────────────┘ Step-by-step trace. Step Decision Reason 1 3 servers, by workload class bound blast radius per class 2 Token-based auth via gRPC interceptor central IdP, no per-server users 3 TLS on all servers regulated data, network not trusted 4 Interactive: FAIR scheduler + minShare per analyst-group fair share for human-facing latency 5 ETL: FIFO scheduler, big executor pool batch jobs run to completion 6 ML: dedicated large-memory executors, no Connect on this server (just JobRunner) UDFs + RDD legacy code 7 Per-session pool routing via spark.conf.set("spark.scheduler.pool", ...) analysts get the right pool automatically 8 Catalog shared across all three (Unity / Iceberg REST) one source of truth for tables After the wiring, every team sees their workload's natural latency profile. Interactive users get sub-second responses; ETL meets its SLA; ML gets the big executors. Output: Server Workload Scheduling Auth connect-interactive notebooks, BI FAIR pools, minShare per group JWT, propagated user_id connect-etl Airflow batch FIFO, large queue JWT, service account connect-ml training jobs (or classic Spark) dedicated executors JWT, ML team SA Why this works — concept by concept: One server per workload class — three servers (interactive / ETL / ML) lets each tune its own executor sizing and scheduler. Cheaper than per-tenant; safer than fully shared. Token-based auth via gRPC interceptor — central IdP issues JWTs; the interceptor validates on every call and binds the user_id to the session. No per-server user database; auditable centrally. TLS on every server — gRPC over plain HTTP/2 is fine for trusted clusters; production multi-tenant deployments need TLS to protect plan + result data on the wire. FAIR + minShare for interactive — guarantees executor slots for human users even under ETL pressure. Fair scheduler's per-pool weight + minShare is the right tool. Cost — three Connect Servers is a 3x increase in cluster fixed cost compared to one server, amortised across the entire org. For 200 users, the per-user cost is still negligible.
