Migration + production patterns Migrating to spark 4 connect is mostly two lines — but the API-gap surface and deployment shape are where senior engineers earn their keep The mental model in one line: migrating a PySpark job from classic to Connect is .master(...) → .remote(...) plus a forbidden-API audit; deploying a Spark Connect Server in production is "long-running JVM with TLS-fronted gRPC, dynamic executor allocation, observability piped to Prometheus, and a fair-scheduler config" . Once you say "two-line migration + API-gap list + long-running server," every production-pattern interview becomes a deduction from those pieces. The migration playbook — order matters. Step 1: Audit forbidden APIs. Grep the codebase for sc.parallelize , sc.accumulator , sc.broadcast , .rdd , JavaRDD , JavaSparkContext . Anything that hits has to be rewritten or stays on classic. Step 2: Audit UDF surface. Plain Python UDFs and Pandas UDFs migrate cleanly. Scala UDFs require uploading a JAR via AddArtifacts (or pre-installing on the server). Java-class-based custom aggregators may not have direct Connect equivalents. Step 3: Swap .master(...) for .remote(...) . Two lines per script. The rest of the code is identical. Step 4: Run integration tests against a local Connect Server. pyspark[connect] ships a local server that runs in-process for tests, eliminating "what if this works on classic but not Connect" surprises. Step 5: Deploy a real Connect Server. Kubernetes / EMR / Databricks all support Spark Connect Server in 2026. Pick the simplest managed offering you have. Step 6: Roll out client by client. Migrate notebooks first (highest interactive value), then small batch jobs, then large ETL. Keep spark-submit for the RDD legacy. Forbidden API checklist (the ones you actually hit). sc.parallelize(...) — replace with spark.createDataFrame(...) . Same end state for small synthetic data. sc.accumulator(...) — rewrite the metric using a SQL aggregation. Accumulators were always a niche pattern; the DataFrame replacement is cleaner. sc.broadcast(...) — for small lookup tables, use broadcast(df) from pyspark.sql.functions . The hint instructs Catalyst to do the broadcast join. Direct broadcast-variable use is gone. df.rdd — most uses of .rdd are for old map / flatMap / reduceByKey patterns. Rewrite as DataFrame operations + mapInPandas / mapInArrow / mapPartitions via a Pandas UDF. Custom partitioners — df.rdd.partitionBy(...) doesn't exist on Connect. Use df.repartition(N, "key") for hash partitioning; for custom partitioning logic, do it server-side via a UDF or pre-stage the data. SparkSession.builder.enableHiveSupport() plus direct HiveContext — Connect uses the same Hive metastore via catalog APIs; the direct HiveContext is not exposed on the client. Deploying a Spark Connect Server on Kubernetes. Use the spark-operator or a plain Deployment running start-connect-server.sh . Configure dynamic allocation ( spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled=true ) so executor count scales with session load. Kubernetes-native executor pods are created and destroyed by the operator. Enable TLS via spark.connect.grpc.server.useTransportSecurity=true and mount certificates from a Secret . Mount the fairscheduler.xml config from a ConfigMap . Expose the server via a Service of type ClusterIP (internal only) or behind a TLS-terminating ingress. Health-check the gRPC port via a tcpSocket probe (gRPC HTTP/2 keeps the connection alive). Deploying on EMR. AWS EMR 7.x supports Spark Connect natively as a configuration option. Set spark.connect.startWithSession=true in the EMR cluster config and the Connect Server starts as part of the cluster bootstrap. The cluster's master node exposes the Connect endpoint (default port 15002); use security groups to allow client connections from the analyst / service VPC subnet. Combine with EMR Studio for managed notebooks that already speak Spark Connect. Deploying on Databricks. Databricks Connect (the Databricks branding for Spark Connect) is the default client model on Databricks Runtime 14+. Each Databricks cluster exposes a Connect endpoint; clients use databricks-connect (a thin wrapper around pyspark[connect] ). Auth is via Databricks personal access tokens or service principals, propagated through the gRPC Authorization header. Unity Catalog ACLs are enforced server-side using the user identity from the token. Performance overhead — what is the cost of Connect vs classic? Plan ship overhead is small (single-digit milliseconds for typical plans). Negligible for any non-trivial query. Arrow encoding overhead is negative for many workloads — Arrow is more efficient than the row-based RDD<InternalRow> path. Results often stream faster on Connect than on classic PySpark (which used row pickling through Py4J). gRPC streaming overhead is dominated by the actual query cost. Within the same data centre, gRPC adds <1 ms per batch over raw TCP. Driver memory pressure is a real difference — the Connect Server holds many sessions, so its driver memory has to be sized for the union of all active sessions. Classic Spark drivers serve one job each. Observability — the production toolkit. Export Spark metrics via the JMX exporter or the built-in Prometheus servlet ( spark.metrics.conf block). Log every ExecutePlan call with (user_id, session_id, plan_summary, duration_ms) for per-session telemetry. Hook Connect interceptors for tracing; OpenTelemetry libraries exist for the gRPC server. Capture the Spark UI URL and surface it in the client (Connect clients can call spark.client.get_attempt_id() to print a UI link). Senior interview signals on production Spark Connect. Do you mention TLS + token auth via gRPC interceptor as a default for any production deployment? — required senior signal. Do you discuss dynamic allocation + scheduler pools to bound noisy-neighbour effects? — senior signal. Do you frame forbidden APIs as a one-time audit, not an ongoing burden (since DataFrame code is the common case)? — senior signal. Do you call out observability across user_id + session_id , not just per-job? — senior signal. Worked example — migrating a forbidden-API hotspot Detailed explanation. A typical migration encounter — a Python script that uses sc.parallelize for a synthetic test fixture, sc.broadcast for a lookup map, and df.rdd.map for a custom transform. Show the rewrites. Question. Migrate a PySpark script that uses three classic-only APIs to a Spark Connect-compatible version. Input. API Used for Connect replacement sc.parallelize synthetic test data spark.createDataFrame sc.broadcast small lookup table broadcast() join hint df.rdd.map per-row transform mapInPandas / SQL expression Code. # Classic — uses sc directly from pyspark.sql import SparkSession spark = SparkSession . builder . master ( " local[*] " ). getOrCreate () sc = spark . sparkContext # 1. synthetic test data rdd = sc . parallelize ([( " a " , 1 ), ( " b " , 2 ), ( " c " , 3 )]) df = rdd . toDF ([ " key " , " value " ]) # 2. broadcast lookup country_map = sc . broadcast ({ " a " : " US " , " b " : " DE " , " c " : " FR " }) enriched = df . rdd . map ( lambda r : ( r . key , r . value , country_map . value . get ( r . key ))) out = enriched . toDF ([ " key " , " value " , " country " ]) # 3. per-row transform doubled = out . rdd . map ( lambda r : ( r . key , r . value * 2 , r . country )). toDF ([ " key " , " v2 " , " country " ]) doubled . show () # Connect — DataFrame-only from pyspark.sql import SparkSession from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast , col , lit , when spark = SparkSession . builder . remote ( " sc://connect:15002 " ). getOrCreate () # 1. synthetic test data — direct DataFrame df = spark . createDataFrame ([( " a " , 1 ), ( " b " , 2 ), ( " c " , 3 )], [ " key " , " value " ]) # 2. broadcast lookup as a small DataFrame + broadcast() hint country_df = spark . createDataFrame ( [( " a " , " US " ), ( " b " , " DE " ), ( " c " , " FR " )], [ " key " , " country " ]) enriched = df . join ( broadcast ( country_df ), on = " key " , how = " left " ) # 3. per-row transform via SQL expression doubled = enriched . withColumn ( " v2 " , col ( " value " ) * 2 ). drop ( " value " ) doubled . show () Step-by-step explanation. sc.parallelize([...]).toDF(...) becomes spark.createDataFrame([...], [...]) . Same in-memory result; no RDD touched. The Connect client serialises the small list as a LocalRelation protobuf node and the server materialises it into a DataFrame. sc.broadcast(...) was the explicit way to ship a small lookup table to executors. With DataFrames, the idiom is "make it a small DataFrame and tell Catalyst to broadcast it via the broadcast() hint." The hint is a planning-time decoration; Catalyst inserts a BroadcastHashJoin instead of a SortMergeJoin . df.rdd.map(lambda r: ...) is the legacy "I have a per-row function" pattern. The DataFrame replacement is either a SQL expression (when the logic is expressible in SQL) or a Pandas UDF (when it needs Python code). The example uses a SQL expression col("value") * 2 because the logic is trivial. For non-trivial per-row Python code, use df.mapInPandas(fn, schema) which executes a Python function over Arrow batches. This is the closest Connect equivalent to df.rdd.map . The migrated script has zero references to sc or rdd . It runs identically on Connect or classic Spark — and Catalyst optimises it better than the RDD version did. Output. key v2 country a 2 US b 4 DE c 6 FR Rule of thumb. Whenever you see df.rdd.<op> in legacy code, first ask whether the operation can be expressed in SQL. If yes, use SQL — it's faster, more readable, and Catalyst-optimisable. If no, use a Pandas UDF. Worked example — Kubernetes deployment of a Spark Connect Server Detailed explanation. A common production probe — "show me a complete Kubernetes manifest for a Spark Connect Server with TLS, dynamic allocation, fair scheduler, and Prometheus metrics." The answer is a multi-resource YAML showing the production wiring. Question. Write a Kubernetes deployment for a Spark Connect Server with the production essentials. Walk through each block. Input. Production requirement YAML resource TLS on gRPC Secret + useTransportSecurity flag Dynamic allocation dynamicAllocation.enabled=true + executor pod template Fair scheduler ConfigMap with fairscheduler.xml Prometheus metrics spark.metrics.conf + ServiceMonitor Code. apiVersion : v1 kind : ConfigMap metadata : name : connect-fair-scheduler data : fairscheduler.xml : | <?xml version="1.0"?> <allocations> <pool name="interactive"> <schedulingMode>FAIR</schedulingMode> <weight>3</weight> <minShare>10</minShare> </pool> <pool name="etl"> <schedulingMode>FIFO</schedulingMode> <weight>1</weight> </pool> </allocations> --- apiVersion : apps/v1 kind : Deployment metadata : name : spark-connect spec : replicas : 1 selector : matchLabels : app : spark-connect template : metadata : labels : app : spark-connect spec : containers : - name : server image : registry/spark:4.0.0 args : - " /opt/spark/sbin/start-connect-server.sh" - " --conf" - " spark.scheduler.mode=FAIR" - " --conf" - " spark.scheduler.allocation.file=/etc/spark/fairscheduler.xml" - " --conf" - " spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled=true" - " --conf" - " spark.dynamicAllocation.minExecutors=2" - " --conf" - " spark.dynamicAllocation.maxExecutors=50" - " --conf" - " spark.connect.grpc.server.useTransportSecurity=true" - " --conf" - " spark.metrics.conf.*.sink.prometheusServlet.class=org.apache.spark.metrics.sink.PrometheusServlet" ports : - { name : grpc , containerPort : 15002 } - { name : prometheus , containerPort : 4040 } resources : requests : { cpu : 4 , memory : 16Gi } limits : { cpu : 8 , memory : 32Gi } volumeMounts : - { name : scheduler , mountPath : /etc/spark } - { name : tls , mountPath : /etc/tls } volumes : - name : scheduler configMap : { name : connect-fair-scheduler } - name : tls secret : { secretName : spark-connect-tls } --- apiVersion : v1 kind : Service metadata : name : spark-connect spec : selector : { app : spark-connect } ports : - { name : grpc , port : 15002 , targetPort : 15002 } Step-by-step explanation. The ConfigMap carries the fairscheduler.xml file; the Deployment mounts it at /etc/spark/fairscheduler.xml and the Connect Server reads it at boot, registering the two pools. The Deployment starts start-connect-server.sh with the Spark configs that turn on FAIR scheduling, dynamic allocation (with sane min/max), TLS on the gRPC server, and the Prometheus servlet. Dynamic allocation auto-scales executor pods between 2 and 50 based on pending task pressure. The Kubernetes scheduler creates and destroys executor pods on demand; idle pods are reclaimed after spark.dynamicAllocation.executorIdleTimeout . TLS certificates come from a Kubernetes Secret (created via cert-manager or manual rotation). The server presents the cert on the gRPC port; clients verify it before issuing ExecutePlan calls. The Service exposes the gRPC port internally. A token-validation gRPC interceptor (configured via spark.connect.authenticate.token ) enforces auth on every call. Prometheus scrapes :4040/metrics (or wherever the Prometheus servlet binds); a ServiceMonitor (not shown) wires this into a Prometheus operator. Output. Concern How it's covered TLS on the wire useTransportSecurity=true + mounted certificate Auto-scaling executor pool dynamic allocation (2-50 executors) Fairness across workloads FAIR scheduler + named pools Observability Prometheus servlet + log shipping Auth gRPC interceptor ( spark.connect.authenticate.token ) Rule of thumb. Production Connect deployments are 80% standard Spark configuration (dynamic allocation, scheduler, metrics) and 20% Connect-specific (TLS on gRPC, auth interceptor, session timeout). If your Spark platform team can run classic Spark in production, they can run Connect with a one-week ramp. Worked example — observability across sessions Detailed explanation. A typical production probe — "how do you monitor a Spark Connect Server with 100 active sessions? Per-session latency, per-user resource use, per-query plan visibility?" The answer combines built-in metrics, custom interceptors, and Spark UI integration. Question. Build an observability layer for a multi-tenant Connect Server: per-session metrics, per-query tracing, and Spark UI links surfaced in the client. Input. Signal Source Sink Per-session execution latency gRPC interceptor Prometheus + Grafana Per-query Spark plan server logs Loki / Elastic Per-query UI link client print notebook output Session count by user_id server registry Prometheus Code. // Example custom gRPC interceptor (Scala, server-side) class ConnectMetricsInterceptor extends ServerInterceptor { override def interceptCall [ Req , Res ]( call : ServerCall [ Req , Res ], headers : Metadata , next : ServerCallHandler [ Req , Res ] ) : ServerCall.Listener [ Req ] = { val start = System . currentTimeMillis () val userId = Option ( headers . get ( USER_ID_KEY )). getOrElse ( "anon" ) val method = call . getMethodDescriptor . getFullMethodName val wrapped = new SimpleForwardingServerCall ( call ) { override def close ( status : Status , trailers : Metadata ) : Unit = { val duration = System . currentTimeMillis () - start Metrics . recordCall ( userId , method , status . getCode . toString , duration ) super . close ( status , trailers ) } } next . startCall ( wrapped , headers ) } } # Client-side helper to log the Spark UI URL for the query import logging spark = SparkSession . builder . remote ( " sc://connect:15002 " ). getOrCreate () df = spark . read . parquet ( " s3://lake/events/ " ) result = df . filter ( " status = ' OK '" ). groupBy ( " user_id " ). count () # Trigger execution result . show () # Print the Spark UI link from the client session metadata print ( f " Spark UI: { spark . client . get_attempt_id () } " ) Step-by-step explanation. The Scala interceptor wraps every gRPC call on the server side. It records (user_id, method, status, duration_ms) to a Prometheus counter + histogram. With this, Grafana dashboards expose per-session, per-method latency and error rate. The server logs every ExecutePlan request with the plan summary at INFO level. Logs are shipped to Loki or Elasticsearch; a saved query like user_id=alice status=ERROR returns the failed queries for a given analyst. For per-query tracing, the interceptor can also push spans to OpenTelemetry. Each ExecutePlan becomes a parent span; child spans for plan analysis, optimisation, execution come from instrumented Spark internals. The client-side spark.client.get_attempt_id() returns the Spark UI URL for the most recent execution. Surfacing this in a notebook lets analysts jump to the Spark UI without searching for the right server / cluster. Per-user dashboards (sessions, queries/minute, avg latency, failure rate) make capacity planning and noisy-neighbour debugging tractable. Without them, a busy multi-tenant Connect Server is a black box. Output. Metric Where to look Per-session p99 query latency Grafana, grouped by user_id Failed queries by user Loki saved query Live session count Prometheus gauge, server registry Per-query plan + UI link server logs + client print Rule of thumb. Multi-tenant Connect Servers without per-user observability are operationally impossible to manage. Treat the metrics interceptor and the log shipper as day-one infrastructure, not nice-to-haves. Senior interview question on production Spark Connect rollout A senior interviewer might frame this as: "You're the tech lead for a Spark Connect rollout across a 500-engineer org with existing PySpark / spark-submit infrastructure. Walk me through the 90-day plan and the risks you'd guard against." Solution Using a phased rollout + risk-mitigation playbook 90-day Spark Connect rollout ============================ Days 0-15 — Discover + audit - Inventory: all PySpark + Scala jobs - Tag each job: DF-only / RDD / accumulator / broadcast / custom partitioner - Identify the 10-20% with hard RDD dependencies — these stay on spark-submit - Survey notebook users: how many sessions, how many concurrent users Days 15-45 — Pilot - Stand up one Connect Server in staging (K8s, TLS, fair scheduler) - Migrate 5 medium-complexity batch jobs and 10 notebooks - Compare wall-clock time, cost, and developer ergonomics vs classic - Build the observability stack (metrics interceptor, logs, dashboards) Days 45-75 — Production rollout - Stand up the production Connect Server with dynamic allocation - Migrate all DF-only PySpark batch jobs (cron / Airflow) - Move notebook fleet over (analyst training session: 2 hours) - Keep classic spark-submit for the RDD legacy Days 75-90 — Hardening - Add per-tenant scheduler pools - Add Connect-specific runbooks for the on-call rotation - Run a load test: 100 concurrent sessions, mixed workload - Sign off on the rollout Step-by-step trace. Phase Risk Mitigation Discover Hidden RDD dependencies break migration exhaustive grep + classify each job Pilot Performance regression on a critical job benchmark with realistic data; rollback plan Rollout Auth misconfig leaks data across sessions TLS + token interceptor day one; pen-test Rollout Noisy neighbour starves interactive users fair scheduler + minShare from boot Hardening Session leak (clients never close) idle timeout + monitoring of session count Hardening Driver OOM under high concurrency size driver memory for max-concurrent sessions Hardening UDF dependency drift between client and server pre-install heavy deps on server; ship light UDFs After the 90-day pass, the org has Connect as the primary DataFrame deployment shape, classic Spark for RDD legacy, and per-tenant observability across both. Output: Verdict Reasoning Adopt Connect as primary for DataFrame + SQL workloads matches notebook + service + Lambda client shapes Keep classic Spark for RDD legacy jobs API gap is permanent for those; cheaper than rewriting Run one Connect Server per workload class bound noisy-neighbour effect across teams Mandate TLS + token auth from day one regulated data; the failure mode is unauditable leaks Why this works — concept by concept: Discover before migrate — the API-gap audit is the single most important investment. Without it, you discover blockers per job at the worst possible time. With it, the migration is mechanical for the 80% and explicit for the 20%. Pilot proves the dev ergonomics — Connect's biggest win is interactive ergonomics (no JVM startup, fast cold start, reconnect-after-crash). Pilot lets analysts feel the difference before the rollout. Production hardening is operational, not API-level — TLS, auth, fair scheduler, dynamic allocation, observability. These are all standard distributed-system patterns applied to the gRPC + Spark stack. Keep classic Spark in the toolkit — Connect is not "the replacement for spark-submit"; it is "the new default for interactive and DataFrame workloads." RDD code stays on classic. Cost — the rollout is 90 days of platform-team focus and a ~10-20% one-time cluster cost during the dual-running period. After completion, ongoing cost is comparable to or lower than the classic-only baseline (slimmer clients, shared cluster). ETL Topic — etl Spark migration + deployment problems Practice → Optimization Topic — optimization Spark performance + tuning problems Practice → Cheat sheet — Spark Connect recipes Quick-start a Connect client. pip install pyspark[connect]==4.0.0 → SparkSession.builder.remote("sc://host:15002").getOrCreate() . No JVM, no SPARK_HOME , no spark-submit . Works in notebooks, Lambdas, CI runners. Quick-start the local Connect Server. ./sbin/start-connect-server.sh --packages org.apache.spark:spark-connect_2.13:4.0.0 — boots a local server on port 15002. Use for integration tests and development. Quick-start spark-shell --remote . spark-shell --remote sc://host:15002 opens a Scala REPL against a remote Connect Server. Same shell, no local JVM cluster. Connect-incompatible API checklist. sc.parallelize → spark.createDataFrame . sc.broadcast → broadcast() join hint. sc.accumulator → SQL aggregation. df.rdd.map → mapInPandas / SQL expression. Custom partitionBy → df.repartition(N, "key") . Multi-tenant Connect Server deploy. Single Deployment + Service, spark.scheduler.mode=FAIR , fairscheduler.xml via ConfigMap, dynamicAllocation.enabled=true , TLS via Secret, token auth via gRPC interceptor, Prometheus servlet on 4040. Per-session scheduler pool. spark.conf.set("spark.scheduler.pool", "interactive") early in the client session. Combine with FAIR scheduler + named pools to give human-facing workloads guaranteed minShare. Arrow batch tuning. spark.connect.grpc.arrow.maxBatchSize=4194304 (4 MB default). Increase for large bulk results; decrease for low-latency .show(5) previews. Inbound message size. spark.connect.grpc.maxInboundMessageSize=134217728 (128 MB default per response). Increase if you regularly stream large single batches; otherwise leave as default. Idle session timeout. spark.connect.session.timeout=60m (default tuned for interactive). Lower for cost-sensitive servers; higher for long-running analytical sessions that should survive notebook restarts. TLS + token auth. spark.connect.grpc.server.useTransportSecurity=true plus a gRPC interceptor implementing token validation. Bind the interceptor via spark.connect.grpc.interceptor.classes=com.example.AuthInterceptor . Python UDF gotcha. UDFs serialise via cloudpickle; keep them light. For heavy NumPy / scikit-learn dependencies, pre-install on the server's Python environment instead of shipping per-query. Lifecycle pattern. A Connect client's spark.stop() only closes the gRPC channel; the server session persists until idle timeout. Plan for client reconnect-after-crash to re-attach to the same session ID. Pandas UDF over Connect. @pandas_udf(StringType()) + Series→Series Python function — identical to classic. Runs in a Python worker on the executor, not on the client. Spark Connect on EMR. Set spark.connect.startWithSession=true in EMR cluster config; the Connect Server starts as part of cluster bootstrap and binds to port 15002 on the master node. Spark Connect on Databricks. Use the databricks-connect wrapper around pyspark[connect] ; auth is a Databricks PAT or service principal; Unity Catalog ACLs apply server-side to every query. Reconnect-friendly session ID. Pass a stable spark.connect.session.id from the client (e.g., notebook UUID) so a kernel restart re-attaches to the same server session, preserving caches. Spark UI per session. spark.client.get_attempt_id() returns the per-attempt UI URL; print it in the notebook so analysts can drill into Spark UI without manual navigation. Frequently asked questions What is Spark Connect and why does it matter? spark connect is the decoupled client-server architecture introduced in Apache Spark 3.4 and hardened through Spark 4.x. It replaces the monolithic JVM driver — where the SparkContext, the Catalyst optimiser, the user code, and the executor scheduler all run in a single process — with two cleanly separated pieces: a thin client (any language with a gRPC + protobuf stub) that builds unresolved logical plans, and a Spark Connect Server that hosts long-running SparkSession s, runs Catalyst + execution on its own executor pool, and streams Arrow record batches back. It matters because it changes the deployment shape: notebooks, IDEs, Lambdas, and microservices can now drive Spark queries without embedding a JVM, multi-language teams converge on one protocol, and session lifecycle is independent of client lifecycle (the kernel can crash and reconnect without losing cached state). For senior interviews in 2026, "Spark Connect is a thin client + remote SparkSession over gRPC" is the load-bearing one-sentence answer. Does Spark Connect work with PySpark? Yes — pyspark connect is the most polished client in the matrix. pip install pyspark[connect]==4.0.0 installs a slim (~10 MB) pure-Python wheel with no JVM dependency. Code looks essentially identical to classic PySpark: from pyspark.sql import SparkSession then SparkSession.builder.remote("sc://host:15002").getOrCreate() instead of .master(...) . Almost all DataFrame, SQL, Pandas-API-on-Spark, Structured Streaming, and Pandas UDF code migrates unchanged. The one class of code that does not migrate is anything touching SparkContext directly — sc.parallelize , sc.accumulator , sc.broadcast , df.rdd.map , custom partitioners. For the common 80% of PySpark code that is DataFrame + SQL + Pandas UDFs, the migration is two lines and the runtime cost is negligible. Spark Connect vs Spark Submit — when do I use which? Use Spark Connect when the client is interactive (notebook, IDE, dashboard), the client environment is slim (Lambda, Cloud Run, edge container), multiple clients share a server (notebook fleet, multi-language services), or the codebase is DataFrame/SQL/Pandas-UDF only. Use spark-submit when the client is a long-running batch JVM with no humans in the loop, the code uses RDD / accumulator / SparkContext APIs that Connect does not expose, or you have a strict batch-driver-lifecycle SLA (jobs must run to completion in a sealed driver process). The two shapes are complementary in 2026, not competitive: most teams end up running a Connect Server for interactive + DataFrame ETL and keeping spark-submit for RDD legacy and large batch jobs. The decision question that decides 95% of cases is "is the client interactive, or is it a batch JVM?" What APIs don't work in Spark Connect? The Connect protocol is DataFrame/SQL-only, so anything that requires direct SparkContext access is unavailable on the client. The concrete forbidden-API list: sc.parallelize (use spark.createDataFrame instead), sc.broadcast (use broadcast() from pyspark.sql.functions as a join hint), sc.accumulator (rewrite as a SQL aggregation), df.rdd.map / .flatMap / .reduceByKey (use mapInPandas , mapInArrow , or a SQL expression), df.rdd.partitionBy with custom partitioner (use df.repartition(N, "key") ), classic Java-RDD APIs ( JavaSparkContext , JavaRDD ), and some legacy Scala-class-of UDF overloads. UDF surface: Python UDFs and Pandas UDFs are first-class; Scala UDFs require uploading a JAR via AddArtifacts ; non-JVM clients (Go, Rust) cannot ship UDFs at all and are SQL-only for custom logic. The migration playbook is a one-time audit, not an ongoing burden. Can I run Spark Connect against EMR or Databricks? Yes to both, and as of 2026 both are first-class. AWS EMR 7.x supports Spark Connect natively: set spark.connect.startWithSession=true in the cluster config and the Connect Server starts as part of cluster bootstrap on the master node (port 15002 by default). Configure security groups to allow client subnets to reach the master. EMR Studio for managed notebooks already speaks Spark Connect. Databricks brands Spark Connect as "Databricks Connect" — pip install databricks-connect gives you a wrapper around pyspark[connect] that handles Databricks PAT / service-principal auth and routes to the cluster's Connect endpoint. Unity Catalog ACLs are enforced server-side using the auth token's identity. On both platforms the client-side code is identical: SparkSession.builder.remote(...).getOrCreate() with the platform-specific endpoint URL. Spark 4 Connect — what's new in 2026? spark 4 connect lands several quality-of-life and capability improvements over the 3.4 GA release. Protocol-level: richer SQL coverage (CALL procedures for Iceberg / Delta admin, more catalog operations), improved Arrow encoding for very large result sets (lower per-batch overhead), better streaming support across the major sources (Kafka, Kinesis, Pulsar) and sinks (Iceberg, Delta, JDBC), and broader UDF coverage including more Pandas UDF variants and arbitrary stateful UDFs in Structured Streaming. Tooling-level: improved interceptor surface for auth + observability, native dynamic allocation hooks for Kubernetes, and shipped templates for the most common production patterns (fair scheduler + TLS + token auth). The "no JVM on client" story is now polished enough that most greenfield production pipelines on Spark 4 default to Connect, with spark-submit reserved for batch ETL and RDD legacy. The pyspark connect developer ergonomics — sub-second cold start, slim wheel, reconnect-after-crash — are the dominant adoption driver in 2026. Practice on PipeCode Drill the ETL practice library → for the Spark-shape, DataFrame-API, and pipeline-design family of probes. Rehearse on ETL medium-difficulty problems → when the interviewer wants deployment-shape depth. Sharpen the optimization library → for the Catalyst, AQE, and Spark-tuning surface that Connect inherits unchanged. Layer the streaming library → for the Structured-Streaming-over-Connect coverage that's increasingly common in 2026 platforms. Stack the SQL practice library → for the logical-plan and SQL-on-Spark probes that pair naturally with the Connect protocol story. For the broader Spark surface, work through Spark Connect questions alongside classic-Spark internals so you can articulate both deployment shapes confidently. Lock in Spark Connect muscle memory Docs explain the protocol. PipeCode drills explain the decision — when Connect beats spark-submit, when an API gap forces a rewrite, when session isolation pays back the platform-team cost. title: Spark Catalyst Optimizer & Adaptive Query Execution (AQE): Plan Internals for 2026
author: Gowtham Potureddi
summary: spark catalyst optimizer is the single most leveraged piece of code in the modern data platform — every Spark SQL query, every DataFrame call, every Delta / Iceberg / Parquet read passes through it before a single byte moves — and the part most engineers never look at until production is on fire. Catalyst is the rule-based + cost-based query planner; spark aqe is the runtime feedback loop that sits on top, rewriting plans between stages when the optimizer's compile-time guesses turn out wrong. Together they are the reason df.join(other).groupBy(...).agg(...) doesn't behave like the naive Spark you remember from 2017. This guide is the senior-DE walkthrough you wished existed the first time an interviewer asked "trace a spark logical plan through to a spark physical plan — name the rules" or "when does adaptive query execution actually win, and when is it just overhead?" It walks through the four Catalyst stages (unresolved → analyzed → optimized → physical), the families of spark optimizer rules (column pruning, predicate pushdown, constant folding, join reorder), the three AQE features that pay rent (coalesce post-shuffle partitions, skew join split, dynamic switch to broadcast hash join), how whole stage codegen compiles whole operator chains into a single JVM method, how spark dynamic partition pruning fires at runtime against a partitioned fact table, and the 5-step EXPLAIN -driven runbook senior engineers run before they ever touch a config knob. Each section pairs a teaching block with a Solution-Tail interview answer — code, a step-by-step trace, an output table, then a concept-by-concept breakdown of why it works. When you want hands-on reps immediately after reading, drill the ETL practice library → , rehearse on medium-difficulty ETL problems → , and sharpen the optimisation axis with the optimization drills → . On this page Why Catalyst + AQE define Spark's 2026 query story Catalyst — analyzer to optimizer to planner Adaptive Query Execution — runtime replanning Reading a Spark plan — EXPLAIN, EXPLAIN FORMATTED, DAG UI Plan-tuning recipes + senior interview signals Cheat sheet — Catalyst + AQE recipes Frequently asked questions Practice on PipeCode 1. Why Catalyst + AQE define Spark's 2026 query story Catalyst is the compile-time optimizer, AQE is the runtime feedback loop — every Spark performance conversation starts at one of those two layers The one-sentence invariant: Catalyst rewrites your DataFrame / SQL into an optimised physical plan before execution; AQE re-rewrites that plan during execution as each shuffle stage reports real row counts back to the driver . Every "Spark is slow" debugging session that doesn't start at one of those two layers is doomed to chase symptoms. The configs, the broadcast hints, the partition-count knobs — all of them are levers on Catalyst and AQE . If you don't know what the optimizer is doing, you are tuning blindfolded. The four axes interviewers actually probe. Rules. Catalyst ships dozens of rule families — column pruning, predicate pushdown, constant folding, projection collapse, boolean expression simplification, join reorder. Each rule is a Rule[LogicalPlan] subclass that runs in batches under a RuleExecutor . Senior engineers can name three to five families unprompted. Plan. Every query produces four plans in order: unresolved logical plan , analyzed plan (schema bound), optimized logical plan (rules applied), physical plan ( SparkPlan operators). df.explain("extended") prints all four; df.explain("formatted") (Spark 3.0+) prints a compact tree with a side table of operator details. Statistics. Cost-based optimization (CBO) needs row counts and column histograms. Without ANALYZE TABLE ... COMPUTE STATISTICS , Catalyst falls back to size-only estimates and often picks the wrong join order. CBO is off by default; senior engineers know when to flip it on. Runtime feedback. AQE is the loop that closes the planner blind-spot. It cannot predict shuffle output sizes; only the actual run can. AQE samples the shuffle map output, coalesces tiny partitions, splits skewed ones, and swaps sort-merge joins for broadcast hash joins when one side comes in smaller than the broadcast threshold. Why "Spark is slow" answers usually miss the optimizer. The bottleneck is almost never the language. It is one of: (a) a misplanned shuffle, (b) a skewed key, (c) a broadcast that didn't fire, (d) a partition-prune that didn't fire, (e) whole stage codegen blocked by a Python UDF. All five are plan-level concerns, visible only in EXPLAIN . "Add more executors" rarely helps a misplanned plan. A 5x cluster with a skewed plan still has one core doing 80% of the work. "Cache the DataFrame" doesn't help if the plan above the cache is wrong; you cached the wrong intermediate. The first move on any "Spark is slow" ticket: open the Spark UI's SQL tab , find the slow query, read its plan, find the stage with the long-tail task. Only then start touching configs. What changed in 2026 — Spark 3.5 / 4.0 era. AQE on by default since Spark 3.2 ( spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true ). New jobs inherit the feedback loop without opt-in. Storage-Partitioned Joins (SPJ) — a Catalyst rule that recognises when two tables share a partition layout and skips the shuffle entirely. Big win on Iceberg + Delta partitioned tables. Spark 4.0 ANSI SQL by default — Catalyst now treats overflow / division-by-zero as errors unless you opt out, surfacing planning-level type coercions earlier. Photon-style codegen in Databricks Runtime — vectorised executors that piggyback on Catalyst's physical plan; outside Databricks, the same physical plan still runs through Spark's whole-stage codegen. CBO + AQE are complementary , not redundant. CBO picks the initial plan; AQE corrects it at runtime. Big tables benefit from both. What interviewers listen for. Do you say "Catalyst at compile time, AQE at runtime" in the first sentence? — senior signal. Do you mention "four plans: unresolved, analyzed, optimized, physical" unprompted? — required answer. Do you bring up "AQE coalesces, splits, and swaps to broadcast" as the three big features? — senior signal. Do you push back on "AQE replaces tuning" with "AQE corrects what the planner couldn't predict; you still set good initial partition counts"? — senior signal. Worked example — same query, two planning eras Detailed explanation. A classic interview prompt: "you have an orders fact table joined to a customers dimension. The customers table is 80MB; the orders table is 4TB partitioned by date. What does Catalyst produce in 2018 Spark vs 2026 Spark with AQE on?" The DataFrame code looks identical. The physical plan, the shuffle, and the runtime behaviour are wildly different. Question. Write a customers.join(orders) aggregation, then list the four planning stages each version of Spark produces, the runtime rewrites AQE can apply, and the resulting stage shape. Input. Table Size Partitioning customers 80 MB none orders 4 TB by order_date (3650 partitions) target one row per (customer_id, month) aggregation Code. from pyspark.sql import functions as F monthly = ( customers . alias ( " c " ) . join ( orders . alias ( " o " ), F . col ( " c.customer_id " ) == F . col ( " o.customer_id " )) . where ( F . col ( " o.order_date " ) >= " 2025-01-01 " ) . groupBy ( " c.customer_id " , F . date_trunc ( " month " , " o.order_date " ). alias ( " month " )) . agg ( F . sum ( " o.amount " ). alias ( " total " )) ) monthly . explain ( " formatted " ) monthly . write . mode ( " overwrite " ). parquet ( " s3://lake/curated/monthly " ) Step-by-step explanation. The DataFrame is not executed yet — Spark builds an unresolved logical plan from the lazy operators. Column names are strings; types are unknown. The analyzer resolves names against the Spark catalog ( customers , orders ), binds types, and emits an analyzed plan . Any missing column would fail here, not at run time. The optimizer applies rule batches. where("order_date >= '2025-01-01'") is pushed through the join into the orders scan ( predicate pushdown ); only customer_id , order_date , amount are read ( column pruning ); the date_trunc("month", ...) is folded into a single Catalyst expression. The result is the optimized logical plan . The planner maps logical operators to physical ones via the Strategy API. Without statistics, the planner usually picks SortMergeJoin . With CBO and accurate stats, the 80MB customers side is small enough to trigger BroadcastHashJoin . AQE-era (Spark 3.2+) runtime feedback: even if the planner missed the broadcast, AQE may observe that the customers shuffle output is < 10MB and rewrite the plan mid-query to a broadcast hash join. AQE also coalesces the post-shuffle partitions on the orders side (from 200 down to ~40 if the data is sparse) and splits any heavily-skewed customer. Output. Plan 2018 Spark 2026 Spark with AQE Logical predicate pushdown, column pruning same Physical (compile-time) SortMergeJoin (no stats) SortMergeJoin (no stats) Runtime rewrite none swap to BroadcastHashJoin, coalesce shuffle, split skew Stage count 4 4, but post-shuffle stage coalesced Wall clock 28 min 6 min Rule of thumb. Catalyst handles the rule-based wins (pushdown, pruning, folding). AQE handles the runtime wins (broadcast swap, coalesce, skew split). Together they explain most of the 2026-vs-2018 throughput improvement on the same physical cluster — no code change required. Worked example — the four logical-to-physical stages Detailed explanation. An interviewer asks: "show me what df.explain('extended') actually prints, in order, and explain each stage." The answer demonstrates whether you understand Catalyst as a pipeline of plan rewrites, not a single optimiser pass. Question. Given a simple df.filter(...).select(...).groupBy(...).count() , walk through the four plans Catalyst prints under df.explain("extended") . Highlight what changes between each stage. Input. Stage of plan What it represents Parsed Logical Plan raw operator tree from the user's DataFrame call Analyzed Logical Plan schema bound; columns resolved; types known Optimized Logical Plan rule batches applied (pushdown, pruning, folding) Physical Plan SparkPlan operators ready to run Code. df = spark . read . parquet ( " s3://lake/raw/events " ) out = ( df . filter ( F . col ( " event_type " ) == " click " ) . select ( " user_id " , " event_time " , " page_url " ) . groupBy ( " user_id " ) . count () ) out . explain ( " extended " ) Step-by-step explanation. Parsed Logical Plan. The user's DataFrame chain is captured as Aggregate -> Project -> Filter -> Relation . Column names are unresolved attribute references; the relation is a placeholder pointing at the Parquet path. Analyzed Logical Plan. The analyzer resolves event_type , user_id , event_time , page_url against the Parquet schema. Each attribute now has a concrete ExprId and DataType . Any unknown column or type-mismatch error fires here. Optimized Logical Plan. Rule batches run. Column pruning drops event_time and page_url (the aggregation only needs user_id ). Predicate pushdown moves the event_type='click' filter under the scan so the Parquet reader can skip row groups. Projection collapse merges adjacent Project nodes. Physical Plan. The planner maps the logical Aggregate to a physical HashAggregate (partial + final). The Filter + Project become Project + Filter operators inside a single whole-stage-codegen block. The Relation becomes a FileScan parquet with the pruned column list and the pushed filter encoded in PushedFilters . Output. Plan Key changes Parsed unresolved attributes, raw tree Analyzed attributes resolved, types bound Optimized pruning + pushdown + projection collapse Physical Scan + HashAggregate + WSCG block Rule of thumb. Run df.explain("extended") whenever you don't trust what Spark is doing. The diff between the Analyzed and Optimized plans tells you which rules fired; the Physical plan tells you which operators will actually run. Worked example — when "Spark is slow" is really "the optimizer was blindfolded" Detailed explanation. A team complains a Spark job is 4x slower than it "should" be. The DataFrame code is clean. The cluster has more cores than ever. The wall clock is still 40 minutes. The fix turns out to be one missing ANALYZE TABLE and one missing AQE flag — Catalyst was picking sort-merge joins with no stats; AQE wasn't on; broadcast wasn't firing. Question. Diagnose a slow Spark job that should be fast. Show the EXPLAIN output, identify the root cause, apply the two fixes, and show the new plan. Input. Symptom Value Cluster cores 256 Job wall clock 40 min Long-tail task duration 22 min Stage count 6 Broadcast hint used no Code. # BEFORE — sort-merge join, no stats, AQE off result = ( spark . table ( " fact_orders " ) . join ( spark . table ( " dim_customers " ), " customer_id " ) . groupBy ( " region " ) . agg ( F . sum ( " amount " )) ) result . explain ( " formatted " ) # Physical plan shows: SortMergeJoin + Exchange hashpartitioning(customer_id, 200) # Long-tail task = skewed customer_id (one VIP with 30% of orders) # FIX 1 — turn AQE on (covers coalesce + skew + broadcast swap) spark . conf . set ( " spark.sql.adaptive.enabled " , " true " ) spark . conf . set ( " spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled " , " true " ) spark . conf . set ( " spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled " , " true " ) # FIX 2 — compute table stats so CBO can pick broadcast for the 60MB dim table spark . sql ( " ANALYZE TABLE dim_customers COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS " ) spark . sql ( " ANALYZE TABLE fact_orders COMPUTE STATISTICS " ) Step-by-step explanation. The original plan shows SortMergeJoin with a hashpartitioning(customer_id, 200) shuffle. Sort-merge join requires a shuffle of both sides; the dim_customers side is tiny but still pays the full shuffle cost. Catalyst's planner picked sort-merge because the dim_customers table had no statistics; without size info, Catalyst defaulted to the safest join (sort-merge) instead of broadcast. The long-tail task is a classic skew symptom: one customer_id (a VIP with 30% of orders) hashes to one reducer; that reducer processes 30% of the data while the other 199 sit idle. Fix 1 (AQE). Once AQE is on, the planner produces a tentative SortMergeJoin, but during execution AQE inspects the shuffle map output, sees the dim_customers side is < broadcast threshold (10MB), and rewrites the running plan to BroadcastHashJoin. AQE also splits the skewed customer's reducer into 5 smaller reducers. Fix 2 (stats). With table statistics, even the compile-time plan picks BroadcastHashJoin for the 60MB dim_customers — so the rewrite is unnecessary. The two fixes are complementary; running both is the production-grade answer. Output. Metric Before After Join strategy SortMergeJoin BroadcastHashJoin Shuffle count 1 (200 partitions) 0 Long-tail task 22 min 90 sec (skew split into 5) Wall clock 40 min 4 min Code changes none required 2 config flags + ANALYZE Rule of thumb. Before you touch executor memory, partitions, or schedulers — turn on AQE, run ANALYZE TABLE , and re-read the plan. 80% of "Spark is slow" tickets disappear at that point. Senior interview question on Catalyst + AQE A senior interviewer often opens with: "Walk me through what happens between the moment a user calls df.write.parquet(...) and the moment data starts hitting object storage. Name the planning stages, name the rule families, name the AQE rewrites — and tell me where most production tuning actually lives." Solution Using the four-stage pipeline + AQE feedback loop End-to-end planning + execution timeline ======================================== 1. User builds DataFrame (lazy) → unresolved logical plan 2. df.write triggers an action → Catalyst pipeline kicks off 3. Analyzer binds schema + types → analyzed logical plan 4. Optimizer applies rule batches → optimized logical plan (column pruning, predicate pushdown, constant folding, projection collapse, boolean expression simplification, join reorder if CBO enabled + stats) 5. Planner maps logical to physical → initial physical plan (Strategy API: SortMergeJoin / BroadcastHashJoin / ShuffleHashJoin / etc.) 6. Whole-stage codegen compiles chains → JVM bytecode per stage 7. AQE wraps the physical plan → AdaptiveSparkPlan root 8. Stage 1 runs → shuffle map output stats fed back 9. AQE rewrites downstream stages → coalesce + skew split + BHJ swap 10. Repeat 8-9 per stage → final stage writes to sink Step-by-step trace. Step Owner Output 1-2 Driver (user code) Unresolved plan 3 Driver (Analyzer) Analyzed plan 4 Driver (Optimizer + Rules) Optimized logical plan 5 Driver (Planner) Initial physical plan 6 Driver (Codegen) Bytecode per WSCG block 7 Driver (AQE wrapper) AdaptiveSparkPlan 8-9 Executors → driver Stage stats → rewrite 10 Executors Sink writes The pipeline distinguishes the four logical plans because each stage refines a different concern: parsing is syntax, analysis is binding, optimization is rule-based rewriting, and planning is operator selection. Codegen + AQE then add the runtime layer. Output: Layer When it acts What it fixes Rules (Catalyst) compile time redundant projections, missed pushdowns, constant exprs Stats + CBO compile time wrong join order, wrong join strategy on big tables Codegen per stage per-record interpreter overhead AQE per shuffle stage tiny partitions, skewed partitions, missed broadcasts Why this works — concept by concept: Unresolved → analyzed → optimized → physical — each refinement has one job. Squashing them into "the optimizer" is the most common misconception; senior engineers name all four. Rule batches in Catalyst — rules run in batches under a RuleExecutor . A batch is "run to fixed point or N iterations." Knowing this explains why some optimisations need multiple passes (predicate pushdown enables projection collapse enables more pushdown). CBO is off by default — most teams discover this after months. Without ANALYZE TABLE , Catalyst is in size-only mode. CBO is a one-line flag; the stats are the work. AQE wraps the physical plan — it does not replace it. The compile-time plan is the starting point; AQE rewrites downstream stages once a shuffle's actual output is observable. Codegen is per WSCG block — Spark fuses adjacent operators with the same shuffle boundary into a single Java method. Python UDFs break the block; Pandas UDFs (Arrow) keep it intact. Cost — planning is O(operators) and happens once per query. Codegen is O(stages) and is cached. AQE rewrite is O(stages × rewrite cost) and pays for itself when the rewrite avoids a full shuffle. Catalyst — analyzer to optimizer to planner spark logical plan becomes a spark physical plan through four stages — rules in the middle do the heavy lifting The mental model in one line: Catalyst is a tree-rewriting compiler — your DataFrame call is a tree, every optimisation is a pattern-match-and-rewrite rule, every plan is a tree, and the rules run in ordered batches until the tree stabilises . Once you say "Catalyst rewrites trees," every spark optimizer rules interview question becomes a deduction from "which rule pattern-matches this fragment and what does it rewrite to?" The four plan trees, in order. Unresolved logical plan. Built directly from the user's DataFrame or SQL. Operators are Project , Filter , Join , Aggregate , Relation . Attribute references are unresolved — they're strings. Types are unknown. Analyzed logical plan. The Analyzer runs a batch of rules that resolve attributes against the Spark catalog ( SessionCatalog + ExternalCatalog ), binds types, expands * projections, resolves function calls, and validates the tree. Output: a tree where every AttributeReference has a concrete ExprId and DataType . Optimized logical plan. The Optimizer runs many rule batches: Operator Optimization , Decimal Optimizations , LocalRelation , Replace Operators , etc. Each rule is a Rule[LogicalPlan] that pattern-matches a sub-tree and returns a rewritten one. Rules run in batches until fixed point or a max-iteration cap. Physical plan. The SparkPlanner maps logical operators to SparkPlan (physical) operators via the Strategy API. One logical Join maps to one of: BroadcastHashJoin , ShuffledHashJoin , SortMergeJoin , BroadcastNestedLoopJoin , CartesianProduct . The choice is driven by side sizes, broadcast threshold, and hints. Rule-based optimization — the families to memorise. Column pruning ( ColumnPruning ). Drops projections you don't need. If the final aggregation uses only user_id and amount , every other column is pruned at the scan. Predicate pushdown ( PushDownPredicates ). Moves Filter operators down the tree, past Project and (when correct) past Join . The lowest landing spot is inside the scan — Parquet / Iceberg / Delta readers can skip row groups using min/max stats. Constant folding ( ConstantFolding ). Evaluates expressions on literals at compile time. 1 + 2 becomes 3 ; length("hello") becomes 5 . Boolean expression simplification ( BooleanSimplification ). x AND TRUE becomes x ; x OR FALSE becomes x ; NOT NOT x becomes x . Projection collapse ( CollapseProject ). Adjacent Project operators are merged into one — df.select("a","b").select("a") becomes df.select("a") . Inferring filters from joins ( InferFiltersFromConstraints ). If a = b after a join and you have a > 10 , Catalyst infers b > 10 and pushes it to the other side. Replace operators. EXCEPT becomes LeftAntiJoin ; INTERSECT becomes LeftSemiJoin . Set operators map to join operators for execution. Cost-based optimization — when stats unlock joins. CBO is off by default — spark.sql.cbo.enabled must be true and spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled to enable join reorder. ANALYZE TABLE t COMPUTE STATISTICS captures row count + size. Add FOR COLUMNS col1, col2, ... to capture per-column histograms. With histograms, Catalyst's JoinReorderDP can pick the best join order using a dynamic-programming algorithm over join graphs (similar to System R). Without CBO, the optimizer uses heuristics: tiny side → broadcast; otherwise sort-merge. The Strategy API — how logical becomes physical. A Strategy is a function LogicalPlan -> Seq[SparkPlan] . The planner tries each strategy in order; the first non-empty result wins. Built-in strategies include: JoinSelection , Aggregation , BasicOperators , FileSourceStrategy , DataSourceV2Strategy . Users can add a custom strategy via spark.experimental.extraStrategies (rare; mostly for connector authors). Spark catalog — where attributes get resolved. The SessionCatalog is the per-session view of available tables, temp views, functions. It delegates to an ExternalCatalog (Hive metastore or Spark's in-memory metastore) for persistent tables. Iceberg and Delta plug in via Catalog extensions — spark_catalog , iceberg , delta . The Analyzer routes name resolution through them transparently. Common interview probes on Catalyst. "Name three rules in Catalyst." — column pruning, predicate pushdown, constant folding (any three). "What is the difference between unresolved and analyzed plans?" — name binding + type resolution against the catalog. "How does Catalyst pick a join strategy?" — JoinSelection strategy looks at size hints, broadcast threshold, side estimates. "What is whole stage codegen and where does it fit?" — physical-plan-time compilation of adjacent operators into a single JVM method; lives below Catalyst, above execution. Worked example — predicate pushdown into a Parquet scan Detailed explanation. A common interview question: "you filter a 10-billion-row Parquet table by date. What does Catalyst do, and how do you know it actually worked?" The answer is predicate pushdown into the file format — and you verify it by looking at PushedFilters in EXPLAIN FORMATTED . Question. Filter a partitioned Parquet table by date and column value. Show what Catalyst pushes down to the reader and how to verify it in the plan. Input. Table Partitioning Row count events (parquet) by event_date 10 B filter event_date >= '2026-06-01' AND event_type = 'click' Code. df = spark . read . parquet ( " s3://lake/raw/events " ) out = df . filter (( F . col ( " event_date " ) >= " 2026-06-01 " ) & ( F . col ( " event_type " ) == " click " )) out . explain ( " formatted " ) Step-by-step explanation. The DataFrame chain is captured as Filter(Relation) in the unresolved plan. After analysis, event_date and event_type are typed. The optimizer runs PushDownPredicates . It recognises that Filter sits directly above a Relation (the Parquet scan) and pushes both predicates into the scan node. event_date >= '2026-06-01' becomes a partition filter . The scan reads only the directories that satisfy it; ~30 partitions out of 3650, a 99% file-list reduction. event_type = 'click' becomes a data filter . It is encoded into PushedFilters: [EqualTo(event_type,click)] . Parquet readers use the page-level min/max statistics to skip row groups where event_type != 'click' . In EXPLAIN FORMATTED , look for PartitionFilters: [...] and PushedFilters: [...] under the FileScan parquet . If a predicate is missing, the optimizer couldn't push it (e.g., a UDF in the filter). Output (relevant slice of EXPLAIN FORMATTED). Field Value Scan FileScan parquet events ReadSchema event_date, event_type, ... (pruned) PartitionFilters [event_date >= 2026-06-01] PushedFilters [EqualTo(event_type, click)] DataFilters [event_type = click] (redundant after pushdown) Rule of thumb. Always run df.explain("formatted") and check PartitionFilters and PushedFilters . A filter that does not appear there is being applied after the read — paying full I/O cost. Worked example — column pruning under a wide table Detailed explanation. A 400-column wide table has one downstream query selecting 3 columns. Without column pruning, the read cost is 400 columns wide. With column pruning, the Parquet reader reads only the 3 column chunks. The DataFrame code looks identical; the plan tells you whether the pruning fired. Question. Show how column pruning works against a wide table. Demonstrate the EXPLAIN slice that proves pruning fired. Input. Table Columns Used columns wide_events 400 3 ( user_id , event_time , page_url ) Code. df = spark . read . parquet ( " s3://lake/wide/events " ) out = ( df . select ( " user_id " , " event_time " , " page_url " ) . groupBy ( " user_id " ) . count () ) out . explain ( " formatted " ) Step-by-step explanation. After analysis, the tree is Aggregate(user_id, count) → Project(user_id, event_time, page_url) → Relation(400 cols) . The optimizer runs ColumnPruning . It walks the tree bottom-up: the Aggregate only uses user_id , so the Project above the Relation can drop event_time and page_url . The pruned Project is then merged into the Relation 's read schema. The FileScan parquet 's ReadSchema shrinks from 400 columns to 1 column ( user_id ). Parquet's columnar layout means only the user_id column chunks are read from object storage. I/O drops from ~400x to 1x of the working set. In EXPLAIN FORMATTED , the ReadSchema field on the scan node shows the pruned schema. If it shows all 400 columns, something blocked the pruning (e.g., SELECT * upstream or a Python UDF that reads the row). Output. Field Value ReadSchema (before pruning) 400 columns ReadSchema (after pruning) [user_id: string] Bytes read per task ~1.5 GB → ~6 MB Rule of thumb. Never write df.select("*") unless you actually need every column. Column pruning is the cheapest 100x speedup in Spark — and the easiest to break. Worked example — join reorder with CBO Detailed explanation. A 3-way join large.join(medium).join(small) runs slowly because Spark joins the two largest tables first, shuffling 4TB of data. With CBO on and statistics computed, Catalyst reorders to large.join(small).join(medium) , joining the smallest dimension first and dramatically reducing intermediate row counts. Question. Show how join reorder works when CBO is enabled and table statistics are computed. Input. Table Row count Size fact_clicks 50 B 4 TB dim_pages 100 K 80 MB dim_users 200 M 12 GB Code. # Enable CBO spark . conf . set ( " spark.sql.cbo.enabled " , " true " ) spark . conf . set ( " spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled " , " true " ) # Compute statistics (one-time, after big writes) spark . sql ( " ANALYZE TABLE fact_clicks COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS " ) spark . sql ( " ANALYZE TABLE dim_pages COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS " ) spark . sql ( " ANALYZE TABLE dim_users COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS " ) # Query written in natural left-to-right order joined = ( spark . table ( " fact_clicks " ). alias ( " f " ) . join ( spark . table ( " dim_users " ). alias ( " u " ), " user_id " ) . join ( spark . table ( " dim_pages " ). alias ( " p " ), " page_id " ) ) joined . explain ( " cost " ) Step-by-step explanation. Without CBO, Catalyst preserves the user's join order: (fact_clicks ⋈ dim_users) ⋈ dim_pages . The first join produces a ~4TB intermediate that must then be re-shuffled to join against dim_pages. With CBO + stats, JoinReorderDP enumerates valid orderings of the 3 tables and picks the one with the lowest estimated output rows × bytes cost. The dynamic-programming algorithm considers 2! = 2 orderings for each subtree; for 3 tables it considers 3 (one per "first join"). It picks (fact_clicks ⋈ dim_pages) ⋈ dim_users — joining the smallest dim first, projecting down to a much smaller intermediate. explain("cost") shows the estimated sizeInBytes and rowCount on each operator. The DP cost is the sum of intermediate sizes across the plan. The reordered plan triggers BroadcastHashJoin against dim_pages (80MB, under threshold) and ShuffledHashJoin or SortMergeJoin against dim_users. Net wall clock drops 5-10x on large fact tables. Output. Join order Intermediate size Wall clock (f ⋈ u) ⋈ p (no CBO) 4 TB 35 min (f ⋈ p) ⋈ u (CBO) 80 GB 4 min Rule of thumb. CBO is free once you have stats. Run ANALYZE TABLE ... COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS after every large write that changes table cardinality by more than ~20%. For tables that never change cardinality, run it once and forget. Senior interview question on Catalyst rule batches A senior interviewer might ask: "Catalyst rules run in batches. Walk me through what a batch is, why batches exist, what RuleExecutor does between iterations, and why some rules need multiple passes to converge." Solution Using RuleExecutor and fixed-point iteration RuleExecutor — the rule-batch engine ==================================== A Batch is: name + List[Rule] + Strategy A Strategy is: - Once → run each rule in the batch exactly once - FixedPoint(N) → run the batch repeatedly until either: (a) the tree no longer changes between iterations (b) we hit max iterations N (default 100) RuleExecutor.execute(plan): for batch in batches: iter = 0 loop: iter += 1 newPlan = plan for rule in batch.rules: newPlan = rule.apply(newPlan) if newPlan equals plan: break // fixed point reached if batch.strategy is Once or iter >= N: break // bailout plan = newPlan return plan Step-by-step trace. Iteration Rule Tree state change 1 PushDownPredicates Filter moves below Project 1 CollapseProject adjacent Projects merge 1 ColumnPruning unused columns dropped from scan 2 PushDownPredicates new opportunity revealed by pruning 2 CollapseProject another Project merges 2 ColumnPruning no change 3 (all rules) no change → fixed point The iteration count for the Operator Optimization batch is typically 2-3 in practice. Each rule enables others; the batch converges when no rule rewrites the tree anymore. Output: Batch (selected) Strategy What it does Substitution FixedPoint(100) CTE substitution, window expression resolution Resolution FixedPoint(100) analyzer batch — name + type binding Operator Optimization FixedPoint(100) pruning, pushdown, folding, collapse Decimal Optimizations FixedPoint(100) decimal precision rewrites Join Reorder Once DP join reorder (CBO only) LocalRelation FixedPoint(100) const-folded tree → LocalRelation Why this works — concept by concept: Batches group related rules — running CollapseProject once and never again would miss the projects that only become adjacent after ColumnPruning . The batch lets the rules interact. FixedPoint until convergence — guarantees the optimizer doesn't leave low-hanging rewrites on the table just because of rule ordering. Idempotence is what makes Catalyst safe. Max iterations bailout — protects against pathological rules that flip-flop the tree. In practice 100 is never reached; 2-5 is typical. Strategy = Once vs FixedPoint — some passes (join reorder) are intrinsically one-shot; running them in a fixed-point loop would re-explore the same DP space. Rules compose via tree-matching — every rule is a partial function over the tree's shape. Composability is what lets new rules ship without redesigning the optimizer. Cost — each iteration is O(tree-size × rule-count). With 2-3 iterations and ~50 rules on a typical query, that's bounded compile-time work — milliseconds for most queries.
